It is especially useful in households or other defined close-contact settings and excludes the primary case from the denominator. Incubation period is time from infection to symptoms, serial interval is time between symptom onset in successive linked cases, and case-fatality rate measures severity rather than transmissibility.

question: Which of the following statements is true about health propaganda?
options: It aids in developing a reflective behaviour
subject: PSM
explanation: Health propaganda characteristically appeals to emotion and promotes a predetermined viewpoint, often using selective or one-way communication. It is typically passively received rather than actively acquired, and it aims at compliance rather than development of reflective, self-directed behavior. Health education ideally starts from learner needs, encourages dialogue and critical understanding, builds skills, and supports voluntary behavior change in an enabling environment. In practice, public-health campaigns may use emotional storytelling ethically, but the content should remain accurate, transparent, proportionate, and nonstigmatizing. The distinction is therefore not that emotion is forbidden in education; it is whether emotion supports informed agency or substitutes manipulation for evidence and choice.

question: Which of the following is a cause of exudative pleural effusion?
subject: Medicine
explanation: Rheumatoid arthritis can cause an exudative pleural effusion through inflammatory pleuritis. The fluid often has very low glucose and pH, high lactate dehydrogenase, and elevated protein and may occur with nodules or other systemic RA manifestations. Heart failure, cirrhosis or liver failure, and nephrotic syndrome more commonly produce transudates through hydrostatic or oncotic mechanisms, although diuresis and mixed pathology can complicate Light-criteria classification in real patients.

question: Involvement of which of the following structures leads to the clinical presentation as shown in the image?
subject: Dermatology
explanation: The red linear streak extending proximally from a distal infected lesion represents acute lymphangitis involving superficial lymphatic channels. It commonly accompanies bacterial skin infection, especially streptococcal infection, and may be associated with tender regional lymph nodes, fever, and systemic toxicity. Superficial thrombophlebitis follows a vein and often feels like a tender cord, whereas deep lymphatic involvement would not produce this visible dermal streaking pattern.

question: An older WHO global monitoring indicator used what reference value for the proportion of gross national product spent on health?
subject: Medicine
explanation: The historical WHO monitoring reference in the question was at least 5% of gross national product spent on health. It was used as a global indicator, but WHO later clarified that it was never formally adopted as a universal recommendation and that there is no single magic spending number applicable to every health system. Current health-financing assessment emphasizes adequate pooled public funding, financial protection, equity, efficiency, service coverage, and country-specific fiscal capacity rather than treating 5% alone as proof of performance.

question: Which of the following tests cannot be used to detect blood stains?
subject: Forensic medicine & toxicology
explanation: Barberio's test cannot be used to identify a blood stain because it detects spermine in seminal material by forming yellow needle-like spermine picrate crystals. Takayama and Teichmann are microcrystal tests based on hemoglobin derivatives, and spectroscopy can identify characteristic hemoglobin absorption patterns. These older presumptive or confirmatory forensic methods must be interpreted with controls and sample condition; modern immunochemical and molecular methods may provide greater specificity for species or individual identification.

question: In the classic Indian RCH record system, at which peripheral health facility is the eligible-couple register maintained by the ANM?
subject: PSM
explanation: In the classic RCH system, the eligible-couple register is maintained at the sub-centre by the auxiliary nurse midwife. It lists currently married couples in which the woman is in the reproductive-age group and supports longitudinal family-planning, maternal-health, immunization, and follow-up work. The register helps estimate contraceptive needs and identify pregnancies and service gaps, but it must be updated rather than treated as a one-time census. Current programmes increasingly use RCH portals and digital records, and local forms or terminology may differ. Confidentiality, respectful counseling, voluntary informed contraceptive choice, and avoidance of coercive target-driven practice are essential regardless of the recording system.

question: A 60-year-old patient with painless hematuria is diagnosed with bladder cancer invading the detrusor muscle. Which listed option is the appropriate definitive local treatment after staging and neoadjuvant systemic therapy when indicated?
subject: Anatomy
explanation: Muscle-invasive bladder cancer generally requires definitive local treatment, and radical cystectomy with pelvic lymph-node dissection is the best answer among these options. Current practice usually adds cisplatin-based neoadjuvant combination chemotherapy for an eligible patient before cystectomy; mitomycin C is not the standard neoadjuvant systemic regimen. Intravesical BCG is used for selected high-risk non-muscle-invasive disease, while radiotherapy is part of a carefully selected bladder-preservation protocol rather than the default isolated treatment.

question: Inquest in a case of custodial death is handled by
subject: Forensic medicine & toxicology
explanation: For a death in police or other court-authorized custody, the mandatory independent inquiry is conducted by a Judicial Magistrate or Metropolitan Magistrate. That was required by section 176(1A) of the CrPC applicable to this 2023 recall, and current BNSS section 196(2) continues mandatory magistrate inquiry in addition to police investigation. An Executive Magistrate may conduct other statutory inquests, including specified suspicious deaths, but custodial-death oversight is not left to police, jail administration, or the executive branch alone.

question: You are administering an 8-month-old infant with vitamin A supplements over the course of 2 consecutive days. What is the recommended dose?
subject: Biochemistry
explanation: An 8-month-old belongs to the 6-12-month age group, for which the treatment dose for clinical vitamin A deficiency or xerophthalmia is 100,000 IU orally per administration. The same age-specific dose is given immediately and again the next day, with another dose at least two weeks later in the standard treatment schedule. This therapeutic schedule should not be confused with periodic population supplementation, and intramuscular therapy is reserved for inability to take or absorb oral vitamin A.

question: A 26-year-old male with a history of respiratory tract infection 4 weeks ago is unable to stand or walk for the past 2 weeks. The weakness is progressive, ascending, and symmetrical. The lower limbs were involved first, followed by the upper limbs. Examination shows areflexia. Pain and proprioception are preserved. What is the probable diagnosis?
subject: Medicine
explanation: Progressive symmetric ascending weakness with areflexia several weeks after a respiratory infection is Guillain-Barre syndrome, most commonly acute inflammatory demyelinating polyradiculoneuropathy. Sensory symptoms may occur but objective sensory loss is often mild; respiratory function, bulbar function, autonomic instability, and forced vital capacity require urgent monitoring. CSF often shows albuminocytologic dissociation after the first week, and treatment is intravenous immunoglobulin or plasma exchange, while corticosteroids alone are ineffective.

question: In the traditional hemorrhagic-shock classification, urine output first begins to fall below normal in which class?
subject: Medicine
explanation: Urine output first falls in traditional class II, or moderate, hemorrhagic shock, as sympathetic vasoconstriction and renin-angiotensin activation reduce renal perfusion and conserve volume. The common teaching range is roughly 20-30 mL/hour in an adult, falling further in class III and becoming negligible in class IV. These classes are approximate and can be altered by age, pregnancy, medication, comorbidity, and prehospital fluids, so resuscitation follows physiology, bleeding control, perfusion, lactate, mental state, and dynamic response rather than one table cell.

question: Identify the type of homicide caused by smothering and traumatic asphyxia.
subject: Forensic medicine & toxicology
explanation: Burking is homicide by combining smothering with traumatic compression of the chest, named after the Burke and Hare murders. Smothering blocks the external air passages while chest compression restricts respiratory movement, so external signs can be subtle despite fatal asphyxia. Garroting is ligature strangulation, bansdola refers to a traditional method of compression using sticks or poles, and mugging in forensic terminology involves arm or forearm pressure around the neck rather than the stated combination.

question: Which vitamin deficiency causes xerophthalmia, a condition which causes dry eyes?
subject: Biochemistry
explanation: Vitamin A deficiency causes xerophthalmia, a spectrum that includes night blindness, conjunctival xerosis, Bitot spots, corneal xerosis, ulceration, and keratomalacia. Retinal is required for rhodopsin and visual phototransduction, while retinoic acid maintains epithelial differentiation and mucin-producing surfaces. Advanced corneal disease is an emergency because structural destruction can become irreversible; the B, C, and D vitamin groups do not produce this characteristic ocular sequence.

question: A 28-year-old breastfeeding mother develops acute focal breast pain, erythema, fever, and malaise. Which listed factor most commonly provides a portal of entry for bacteria in lactational mastitis?
subject: ObGy
explanation: A cracked or traumatized nipple can provide a portal of entry for skin organisms, particularly Staphylococcus aureus, in lactational mastitis. Milk stasis from ineffective attachment, missed feeds, oversupply, abrupt weaning, or localized pressure is also central to the inflammatory process, so management includes effective breast emptying, correction of feeding technique, analgesia, hydration, and continued breastfeeding or expression unless a specific contraindication exists. Antibiotics are indicated when bacterial mastitis is likely, symptoms are significant or persistent, or the patient is systemically unwell, with local resistance and MRSA risk considered. A fluctuant or nonresolving mass requires ultrasound assessment for abscess or phlegmon.

question: A person is exempt from criminal responsibility if, because of unsoundness of mind at the time of the act, the person could not know the nature of the act or that it was wrong or contrary to law. Which insanity rule does this reflect?
subject: Forensic medicine & toxicology
explanation: The formulation reflects the M'Naghten rule: because of disease or unsoundness of mind at the time of the act, the accused did not know the nature of the act or did not know that it was wrong or contrary to law. India now states the substantive exemption in section 22 of the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita; the prior equivalent was IPC section 84. Durham focuses on whether the act was a product of mental disease, while irresistible-impulse and Currens formulations use different tests of control or capacity.

question: Which of the following is not true about congenital hypertrophic pyloric stenosis?
options: Caused by hypertrophy of longitudinal muscles
USG is very sensitive in diagnosing this condition
subject: Pediatrics
explanation: Congenital hypertrophic pyloric stenosis is caused predominantly by hypertrophy and hyperplasia of the circular pyloric muscle, so the statement attributing it to longitudinal muscle is false. Infants typically develop progressive projectile nonbilious vomiting after an initial symptom-free period because obstruction lies proximal to the duodenum. Repeated gastric losses can produce hypochloremic, hypokalemic metabolic alkalosis and paradoxical aciduria. Ultrasound is the preferred diagnostic test and measures pyloric muscle thickness and channel length, interpreted with local validated thresholds and clinical findings. Initial treatment corrects dehydration and electrolyte and acid-base abnormalities; pyloromyotomy follows stabilization. The operation splits the hypertrophied circular muscle without opening the mucosa.

question: A person with chronic alcohol use develops acute severe epigastric pain radiating to the back with nausea and vomiting. Serum lipase is more than three times the upper reference limit. What is the most likely diagnosis?
subject: Medicine
explanation: Typical severe epigastric pain radiating to the back plus lipase more than three times the upper reference limit satisfies two standard criteria for acute pancreatitis. Alcohol and gallstones are common causes, but triglycerides, calcium, drugs, trauma, ERCP, and other etiologies should be assessed; early management emphasizes goal-directed fluids, analgesia, nutrition, and severity monitoring. Cholecystitis localizes more to the right upper quadrant, hepatitis produces a hepatocellular pattern, and appendicitis does not explain the pancreatic enzyme elevation.

question: In which poisoning is cherry red hypostasis seen on post-mortem examination?
subject: Forensic medicine & toxicology
explanation: Cherry-red or bright pink postmortem lividity is classically associated with carbon monoxide poisoning because carboxyhemoglobin imparts a characteristic color to blood and tissues. The finding is suggestive rather than independently diagnostic and should be confirmed by toxicologic measurement in the exposure context; refrigeration can also produce pink lividity, and cyanide may cause unusually bright red blood. Hydrogen sulfide can darken tissues, while phosphorus has different gastrointestinal and hepatic manifestations.

question: All of the following changes occur in chronic starvation except?
subject: Medicine
explanation: Hyperthermia is not a typical change of chronic starvation; reduced metabolic rate, loss of insulating fat, impaired thermogenesis, and poor substrate availability favor hypothermia. Prolonged starvation also causes weakness and exhaustion, bradycardia, hypotension, organ atrophy including cardiac muscle, immune dysfunction, endocrine adaptation, and micronutrient deficiencies. Refeeding must be cautious in severe malnutrition because insulin-driven shifts of phosphate, potassium, magnesium, and fluid can precipitate arrhythmia, respiratory failure, neurologic injury, and edema.

question: For how long does a correctly placed CuT 380A provide approved contraceptive protection in the Indian national programme?
subject: ObGy
explanation: CuT 380A provides approved contraceptive protection for 10 years in the Indian national family-planning programme. This is the in-use duration after correct insertion, not the unopened device's shelf life. Copper released from the device impairs sperm function and fertilization, and effectiveness begins immediately. Before insertion, pregnancy and contraindications must be excluded, uterine anatomy assessed, informed consent obtained, and aseptic technique used. The user should be counseled about heavier or more painful menses, warning symptoms, thread awareness, and follow-up when needed. Some evidence and international guidance support effectiveness beyond 10 years in selected circumstances, but the recalled programme answer is 10 years.

question: A public witness of the incident is asked to attend the court and record his evidence. Which type of evidence is provided by the witness to the court?
subject: Forensic medicine & toxicology
explanation: A witness who personally appears in court and states facts perceived directly gives oral evidence. Hearsay is an out-of-court statement offered to prove its contents and is generally restricted subject to statutory exceptions; circumstantial evidence is an inferential category, not determined simply by speaking in court. A subpoena or summons is the legal process compelling attendance or production of material and is not itself evidence.

question: A 73-year-old male smoker with a past history of coronary artery disease presents with blackening of the toes. An image of the foot is shown below. What is the most likely diagnosis?
subject: Anatomy
explanation: In an older smoker with established coronary atherosclerotic disease, ischemic blackening of a toe is most consistent with atherosclerotic peripheral arterial disease or an atheroembolic manifestation. Buerger disease is a nonatherosclerotic segmental inflammatory disorder classically affecting younger heavy tobacco users. Raynaud disease produces episodic, reversible color change from vasospasm, and giant-cell arteritis affects medium and large arteries, typically causing cranial symptoms rather than isolated ischemic toes.

question: A 50-year-old woman has undergone total thyroidectomy for papillary thyroid carcinoma. Pathology and postoperative assessment place her in a group selected for radioiodine evaluation, and the team needs to map iodine-avid remnant or metastatic tissue before planned therapy. Which listed investigation is appropriate?
subject: Medicine
explanation: In the deliberately selected post-thyroidectomy patient being evaluated for radioiodine, a diagnostic radioiodine scan can map iodine-avid remnant or metastatic tissue and support theranostic planning. Current practice is risk-adapted: many low-risk papillary cancers do not need radioiodine or routine diagnostic whole-body scanning and are followed with pathology, neck ultrasound, thyroglobulin and antibodies, and TSH-directed therapy. External-beam radiotherapy and chemotherapy are not routine next steps for differentiated iodine-avid disease, while simple observation is inappropriate in the stated selected setting without completing assessment.

question: Which finding most specifically describes acute sympathomimetic cocaine intoxication rather than a chronic local or neuropsychiatric complication of repeated use?
options: Dilated pupils, rapid pulse and sweating
Hallucination of insects crawling on the skin (formication)
subject: Psychiatry
explanation: Mydriasis, tachycardia, and sweating describe acute cocaine sympathomimetic intoxication rather than a chronic local complication. Repeated intranasal use can cause ischemic septal and palatal perforation, smoked crack cocaine is associated with severe dental and oral disease, and chronic or heavy stimulant exposure can cause formication, excoriations, paranoia, and hallucinations. These categories overlap in real patients, so the distinction is temporal rather than absolute. Cocaine also increases risk of myocardial ischemia, arrhythmia, aortic catastrophe, stroke, seizures, hyperthermia, and rhabdomyolysis. Management of acute toxicity centers on benzodiazepines, temperature control, cardiac and neurologic evaluation, and complication-specific therapy; long-term care needs evidence-based treatment for stimulant-use disorder and comorbidity.

question: Confidentiality is maintained in which of the following conditions?
subject: Medicine
explanation: Confidentiality is a core feature of individual counseling because a person must be able to disclose sensitive information with clear limits on who can access it. The counselor should explain exceptions such as imminent serious harm, mandatory reporting, safeguarding, court order, or consented team sharing and should document only necessary information. Group discussions, panels, and seminars involve multiple participants and cannot promise the same individualized privacy, although respectful handling of personal information remains expected in every setting.

question: A man with prostate cancer has a bone scan showing diffusely intense, relatively symmetric skeletal tracer uptake with faint renal and background soft-tissue activity. In this clinical context, what most likely produces the superscan appearance?
options: Diffuse osteoblastic metastases throughout the skeleton
Tracer uptake confined to the primary prostate tumor
A solitary lytic metastasis in one vertebra
Failed tracer injection with absent skeletal uptake
subject: Orthopedics
explanation: In a patient with prostate cancer, the superscan pattern is most likely caused by diffuse osteoblastic skeletal metastases. Widespread avid bone turnover extracts so much Tc-99m-labeled diphosphonate that the entire skeleton appears intensely visible while renal, bladder, and soft-tissue activity becomes faint. The appearance can also occur with high-turnover metabolic bone disease, but the clinical context and metastatic distribution determine the interpretation. It is not uptake by the primary prostate lesion itself, nor does a single lytic metastasis produce a skeletal superscan. The kidneys are faint because relatively little tracer remains for excretion, not because absent renal function is required. A superscan can mask individual lesions, so comparison with CT, PSA, symptoms, and other imaging is important. PSMA PET may offer additional staging information but is a different tracer mechanism.

question: A 50-year-old factory worker was brought to the emergency room with complaints of headache, vomiting, and blurring of vision after he consumed local spirit. Which of the following is used for the treatment of his condition?
subject: Pharmacology
explanation: Visual blurring after ingestion of adulterated local spirit strongly suggests methanol poisoning. Fomepizole inhibits alcohol dehydrogenase and prevents formation of formaldehyde and formic acid, the metabolites responsible for severe anion-gap acidosis, retinal injury, and basal-ganglia toxicity. Ethanol is an alternative competitive substrate when fomepizole is unavailable. Management also includes airway and supportive care, bicarbonate for significant acidosis, folinic or folic acid to enhance formate metabolism, serial osmolality and acid-base monitoring, and hemodialysis for severe acidosis, visual or neurologic toxicity, renal failure, or high concentrations. Naloxone reverses opioids, flumazenil selected benzodiazepine toxicity with major cautions, and N-acetylcysteine treats acetaminophen poisoning.

question: A 55-year-old patient with hematuria has the shown left renal mass containing macroscopic fat and prominent intratumoral vessels on CT. What is the most likely diagnosis?
subject: Radiology
explanation: The renal mass contains macroscopic fat and prominent vessels, making angiomyolipoma the most likely diagnosis. Angiomyolipoma is a benign mesenchymal tumour composed of variable amounts of blood vessels, smooth muscle, and adipose tissue. Macroscopic fat on CT is highly characteristic, although rare fat-containing renal cell carcinomas and fat-poor angiomyolipomas require careful evaluation. Symptoms and hemorrhage risk depend on aneurysms, vascularity, size, growth, pregnancy, and tuberous-sclerosis-complex association rather than a single universal diameter cutoff. Management ranges from surveillance to selective arterial embolization, nephron-sparing intervention, ablation, or mTOR inhibition in selected TSC-associated disease. Acute retroperitoneal bleeding requires stabilization and usually urgent embolization. A simple cyst lacks enhancing vessels and solid components.

question: Under the Employees' State Insurance rules applicable to an insured woman with fewer than two surviving children, what is the standard maternity-benefit period for confinement?
options: Sixteen weeks, all beginning after confinement
Twenty-eight weeks, with twelve weeks before confinement
Twenty-six weeks, with up to eight weeks before confinement
Thirty-six weeks, beginning when pregnancy is certified
subject: ObGy
explanation: Under the cited Employees' State Insurance maternity-benefit provision, an eligible insured woman with fewer than two surviving children receives 26 weeks for confinement, and no more than eight weeks may precede the expected date of confinement. The wording is permissive: benefit does not have to begin exactly eight weeks before delivery. Different durations apply in specified situations, including an insured woman with two or more surviving children and commissioning or adoptive mothers, and separate benefits exist for miscarriage or pregnancy-related sickness. Eligibility and payment depend on the ESI Act, regulations, contribution conditions, certification, and claims process, so maternity benefit should not be reduced to a generic private-employment leave rule.

question: Which listed whole-body functional imaging modality is preferred for staging a well-differentiated somatostatin-receptor-positive neuroendocrine tumour?
options: Contrast-enhanced magnetic resonance imaging with diffusion
subject: Radiology
explanation: Somatostatin-receptor PET/CT, such as 68Ga-DOTATATE PET/CT, is preferred whole-body functional imaging for many well-differentiated somatostatin-receptor-positive neuroendocrine tumours. It improves lesion detection compared with older somatostatin-receptor scintigraphy and is used for staging, finding an unknown primary, evaluating suspected recurrence, and determining receptor expression before peptide-receptor radionuclide therapy. Multiphasic CT and contrast MRI remain essential for anatomic characterization, liver assessment, surgical planning, and response evaluation. Tumour biology matters: poorly differentiated neuroendocrine carcinoma often loses SSTR expression and has higher glycolytic activity, making 18F-FDG PET more informative. Generic PET is therefore not a complete answer; tracer, grade, differentiation, Ki-67, clinical question, and prior imaging determine the correct study.

question: A patient has biochemically confirmed acromegaly from a pituitary somatotroph adenoma and has persistent active disease after surgery, or cannot undergo surgery. Which listed drug is an appropriate long-acting first-generation somatostatin-receptor ligand?
subject: Pharmacology
explanation: Lanreotide depot is a long-acting somatostatin-receptor ligand that suppresses growth-hormone secretion and can normalize IGF-1 or shrink a somatotroph adenoma in persistent postoperative disease or when surgery is not feasible. Transsphenoidal surgery is usually preferred first for a resectable pituitary adenoma, so the treatment sequence in the repaired stem matters. Octreotide LAR is a similar first-generation option. Pegvisomant blocks the GH receptor and is especially effective for IGF-1 control; cabergoline helps selected mild disease, and pasireotide can work after first-generation resistance but increases hyperglycemia risk. Therapy is guided by tumor anatomy, symptoms, IGF-1, comorbidity, glucose status, and residual mass rather than one drug for every patient.

question: Which presentation, in the absence of any additional high-risk family history, ancestry, tumor subtype, or personal cancer history, is least likely by itself to indicate BRCA1/BRCA2 germline testing?
options: A single unilateral breast cancer diagnosed at age sixty-five
Male breast cancer diagnosed at any age
Bilateral primary breast cancers in the same patient
Breast cancer together with epithelial ovarian cancer
subject: ObGy
explanation: An isolated unilateral breast cancer diagnosed at age 65, with no relevant family history, high-risk ancestry, tumor subtype, bilateral disease, or other associated cancer, is the least compelling presentation by itself for BRCA1/BRCA2 testing among these choices. Male breast cancer, bilateral primary breast cancers, and the combination of breast with epithelial ovarian cancer are classic hereditary breast-ovarian cancer warning patterns. Age alone is not an absolute exclusion, however. Modern germline-testing criteria have broadened and also consider triple-negative disease, pancreatic or prostate cancer in the family, Ashkenazi Jewish or other founder ancestry, metastatic treatment implications, and multigene panels. Testing should follow informed counseling and current local eligibility criteria.

question: Which of the following is a first-line antitubercular drug prescribed in the initial 2 months of treatment?
subject: Pharmacology
explanation: Ethambutol is a first-line antitubercular drug included with isoniazid, rifampicin, and pyrazinamide during the standard initial intensive phase for drug-susceptible pulmonary TB under the regimen assumed by the question. It inhibits arabinosyl transferases involved in mycobacterial cell-wall arabinogalactan synthesis. Optic neuritis with impaired visual acuity and red-green discrimination is the important dose-related toxicity, so visual assessment and renal dose adjustment matter. Streptomycin is now reserved for selected indications rather than routine first-line use; linezolid and levofloxacin are important in drug-resistant regimens. Treatment should follow current national program guidance, rapid susceptibility testing, weight-based fixed-dose combinations, adherence support, and disease-specific duration.

question: A 51-year-old lady presents with complaints of a mass in the right breast. On examination, the mass was larger than 5 cm and ipsilateral mobile axillary lymph nodes were present. What is the staging, considering the mass is malignant?
subject: Dermatology
explanation: A breast tumor larger than 5 cm is clinical T3 if it does not directly invade chest wall or skin. Mobile ipsilateral level I or II axillary nodes are clinical N1, and absence of distant metastasis is M0, giving cT3N1M0. T4 requires skin ulceration or edema, satellite nodules, or chest-wall invasion rather than size alone; the prefix yp is used for pathologic staging after neoadjuvant therapy, not the initial clinical assessment.

question: Which statement best describes the daily oxygen pattern in the upper zone of a facultative oxidation pond?
options: Uniformly aerobic conditions at all depths throughout day and night
Uniformly anaerobic conditions at all depths throughout day and night
Daytime surface aeration from algal photosynthesis with oxygen decline at night
Daytime surface anaerobiosis followed by aeration only at night
subject: PSM
explanation: In the upper zone of a facultative oxidation pond, algal photosynthesis supplies oxygen during daylight, supporting aerobic bacterial oxidation of organic matter. At night photosynthesis stops while organisms continue consuming oxygen, so dissolved oxygen falls and the surface may become poorly oxygenated or anaerobic. The deeper sludge zone remains predominantly anaerobic, where settled solids are digested; an intermediate facultative zone contains organisms able to function with or without oxygen. Thus the entire pond is not uniformly aerobic by day and anaerobic by night. Performance depends on sunlight, depth, loading, retention time, temperature, wind mixing, algal balance, and prevention of short-circuiting, odor, and vector problems.

question: Which regional lymph-node basin is a common early site of lymphatic spread from prostate cancer?
options: Obturator and internal-iliac pelvic lymph nodes
Popliteal and deep inguinal lymph nodes
subject: Surgery
explanation: Prostate cancer commonly spreads first to regional pelvic nodes, particularly obturator and internal iliac nodal chains, with external iliac, presacral, and common iliac involvement depending on disease extent and staging definitions. Superficial inguinal nodes are not a usual early drainage basin and suggest unusual distal urethral, penile, scrotal, perineal, or very advanced pathways. The original options were defective because internal iliac nodes are themselves pelvic nodes. Modern nodal assessment uses risk stratification and imaging such as PSMA PET/CT in appropriate settings, while extended pelvic lymph-node dissection remains the most accurate surgical staging method when indicated. Nodal pattern does not replace histology, PSA, grade group, local stage, metastatic evaluation, life expectancy, and patient preferences in treatment planning.

question: Which of the following is true regarding the single exposure point source epidemic?
options: Has more than one incubation period
subject: PSM
explanation: A single-exposure point-source epidemic is usually explosive: susceptible people are exposed over a brief interval and cases rise rapidly, cluster within one incubation period, form a single relatively sharp peak, and decline more gradually. The width of the curve reflects the incubation-period distribution and exposure timing. A continuous common source produces a prolonged plateau or broad curve, an intermittent source produces irregular separated peaks, and a propagated outbreak can show successive waves roughly one incubation period apart. An epidemic curve is interpreted together with person, place, exposure history, laboratory evidence, and case finding; a textbook shape suggests but does not prove a transmission mechanism.

question: Which of the following procedures has the highest risk of causing the recurrence of duodenal ulcers?
subject: Dermatology
explanation: Among classic definitive acid-reducing operations, highly selective or parietal-cell vagotomy preserves antral and pyloric innervation and causes fewer dumping and diarrhea problems but historically had the highest ulcer-recurrence rate, partly because incomplete parietal-cell denervation is technically possible. Truncal vagotomy and more radical resection reduce recurrence more at the cost of greater morbidity. Today most duodenal ulcers are treated medically with proton-pump inhibition, Helicobacter pylori eradication, and removal of ulcerogenic factors, so elective vagotomy is rarely required.

question: Elective splenectomy is preferred in which of the following conditions?
subject: Pathology
explanation: Elective splenectomy is an established treatment for selected patients with hereditary spherocytosis because the spleen preferentially traps and destroys poorly deformable spherocytes. It markedly reduces hemolysis, anemia, jaundice, and pigment-gallstone risk, although it does not correct the inherited red-cell membrane defect and spherocytes remain on the blood film. Splenectomy is generally reserved for severe disease or troublesome moderate disease rather than every patient, and it is usually deferred until after early childhood when possible. Before surgery, vaccination against encapsulated organisms and counseling about lifelong overwhelming postsplenectomy infection risk are essential; thrombosis risk also deserves attention. It is not routine therapy for G6PD deficiency or paroxysmal nocturnal hemoglobinuria.

question: Sickness absenteeism of workers in the industry is used to evaluate
subject: Medicine
explanation: Sickness absenteeism is used as an occupational-health indicator of worker health because patterns of absence reflect illness frequency, severity, workplace exposure, psychosocial conditions, and access to care. Interpretation needs the diagnosis, duration, job category, seasonal patterns, certification practices, organizational culture, and presenteeism because absence is not a pure biological measure. It may also reveal environmental problems, but the direct unit being evaluated by this indicator is morbidity and functional impact among workers.

question: A child's incisors developed opaque white patches during eruption that later became mottled brown. Excessive exposure to which substance during enamel formation causes this?
subject: Pediatrics
explanation: The enamel changes describe dental fluorosis from excessive fluoride exposure while permanent teeth were developing. Mild fluorosis causes fine opaque white lines or patches; increasing exposure can produce mottling, brown staining, pitting, and fragile enamel. The risk depends on dose, duration, timing, water intake, climate, and fluoride from drinking water, swallowed toothpaste, supplements, or other sources. After enamel formation is complete, fluoride does not create developmental fluorosis in that tooth. Fluoride at appropriate concentration helps prevent caries, so the issue is chronic excess rather than fluoride being inherently harmful. Assessment should identify the exposure source and distinguish fluorosis from early caries, enamel hypoplasia, amelogenesis imperfecta, and tetracycline staining.

question: What is the fluid of choice for shock in a child with severe acute malnutrition?
subject: Pediatrics
explanation: In a child with severe acute malnutrition and true shock, the best listed fluid is Ringer lactate with 5% dextrose, given cautiously according to the severe-malnutrition protocol. Hypoglycemia is common, sodium handling is altered, and cardiac reserve may be limited, so standard rapid pediatric shock boluses can cause fluid overload and death. Older WHO protocols also list half-strength Darrow solution with 5% dextrose where available. Shock must be distinguished from uncomplicated dehydration because signs overlap in malnutrition; oral or nasogastric rehydration using the recommended formulation is preferred when shock is absent. Close reassessment of pulse, respiratory rate, perfusion, urine output, mental state, glucose, and signs of overload is essential, with blood transfusion considered in selected severe anemia or septic shock contexts.

question: During neonatal resuscitation, positive-pressure ventilation is initiated for a term infant. What oxygen concentration should generally be used initially?
subject: Pediatrics
explanation: Positive-pressure ventilation for a term newborn should generally begin with room air, containing 21% oxygen. Heart rate and preductal oxygen saturation are then monitored, and oxygen is titrated to age-specific saturation targets because healthy newborn saturation rises gradually over the first minutes after birth. Starting every term infant at 100% oxygen increases oxidative exposure without benefit. Different starting concentrations may be considered for preterm infants according to current resuscitation guidance and local resources, and 100% oxygen is used during chest compressions before titration after circulation improves. Effective ventilation that moves the chest remains the most important intervention for neonatal bradycardia; oxygen concentration cannot compensate for an obstructed airway or ineffective mask ventilation.

question: An 8-year-old child from a malaria-endemic area presents with sudden high-grade fever, generalized seizures, and unarousable coma. Examination shows pallor and hypoglycemia without neck stiffness or a focal neurological deficit; peripheral blood microscopy demonstrates Plasmodium falciparum parasitemia. What is the most likely diagnosis?
subject: Pathology
explanation: This is cerebral malaria: unarousable coma and seizures in a patient with Plasmodium falciparum parasitemia after other causes of encephalopathy have been assessed. Anemia and hypoglycemia are recognized severe-malaria manifestations, and focal neurological signs or meningeal irritation may be absent. A positive smear alone does not prove that coma is caused by malaria in a high-transmission setting, so clinicians still check glucose immediately and evaluate for bacterial meningitis, sepsis, toxins, metabolic disease, and intracranial pathology as indicated. Cerebral malaria requires urgent parenteral artesunate with supportive management, followed by a complete oral artemisinin-based combination regimen once tolerated. Seizures, airway protection, fluids, fever, anemia, and recurrent hypoglycemia require close monitoring.

question: A child who has overdosed on iron tablets comes with nausea and abdominal pain. What is the antidote for this toxicity?
subject: Pharmacology
explanation: Deferoxamine chelates ferric iron and is the antidote for significant acute iron poisoning with systemic toxicity, severe acidosis, shock, altered mental status, or a sufficiently high serum iron concentration in the appropriate post-ingestion window. The ferrioxamine complex is excreted in urine and can produce a vin-rose color, although absence of that color does not indicate failure. Initial priorities include airway, circulation, glucose, acid-base assessment, abdominal radiography for radiopaque tablets, serial iron levels, and whole-bowel irrigation for selected substantial ingestions; activated charcoal does not bind iron effectively. Prolonged or excessive deferoxamine can cause hypotension, pulmonary toxicity, and Yersinia infection. EDTA, dimercaprol, and penicillamine chelate other metals.

question: A patient presents with complaints of sudden onset headache, palpitation, and profuse sweating. The patient has had multiple similar episodes in the past. During these episodes, the patient's blood pressure is elevated. A 24-hour urinary metanephrine is elevated. What is the most likely diagnosis?
subject: Medicine
explanation: Episodic headache, palpitations, sweating, paroxysmal hypertension, and elevated metanephrines indicate pheochromocytoma or a sympathetic paraganglioma. Biochemical confirmation uses plasma free or urinary fractionated metanephrines under appropriate collection conditions, followed by localization imaging and genetic consideration. Preoperative treatment begins with alpha blockade and volume repletion before any beta blocker is added for persistent tachycardia; giving beta blockade first can leave unopposed alpha stimulation and precipitate a hypertensive crisis.

question: After at least 30 seconds of effective positive-pressure ventilation that moves the chest during neonatal resuscitation, below what heart rate should chest compressions be initiated?
subject: Pediatrics
explanation: Chest compressions are initiated when the neonatal heart rate remains below 60 beats per minute after at least 30 seconds of effective positive-pressure ventilation that visibly moves the chest. Ventilation is the priority because neonatal bradycardia is usually caused by inadequate gas exchange. Before compressions, the team corrects mask seal, airway position, obstruction, and ventilation pressure and considers an advanced airway. Compressions are coordinated with ventilation in a 3:1 ratio, using 100% oxygen during the compression phase, with reassessment after an appropriate interval. A heart rate below 100 indicates a need for effective ventilation but not immediate compressions; starting compressions before establishing ventilation delays the intervention most likely to reverse the bradycardia.

question: In Scammon's growth curves, the neural pattern most closely represents growth of which organ?
subject: Medicine
explanation: Scammon's neural growth curve most closely represents growth of the brain and related neural structures. Neural tissues grow rapidly in early childhood and approach near-adult size well before adolescence, unlike the general somatic curve of height and skeletal mass or the lymphoid curve that overshoots adult size before involution. Dental development has its own timing and is not the organ pattern named by the neural curve.

question: A 70-year-old smoker has high fever, dry cough, confusion, diarrhea, bilateral lower-zone infiltrates, hyponatremia, and mildly elevated transaminases. Sputum Gram stain shows many leukocytes but no visible organism. Which pathogen is most likely?
subject: Microbiology
explanation: High fever, relative extrapulmonary features such as confusion and diarrhea, hyponatremia, mild transaminase elevation, and pneumonia with many inflammatory cells but no organism on routine Gram stain strongly suggest Legionella pneumophila. It is a faintly staining intracellular Gram-negative bacillus acquired from aerosolized water systems rather than usually transmitted person to person. Urinary antigen rapidly detects L. pneumophila serogroup 1, while culture on buffered charcoal yeast-extract agar permits broader detection and epidemiology; respiratory PCR is increasingly useful. Treatment requires an intracellularly active agent such as azithromycin or levofloxacin, selected according to severity, patient factors, interactions, and local guidance.

question: A 6-year-old child is short but grows at a normal annual velocity. Height is appropriate for the mid-parental target range, and bone age equals chronological age. What is the most likely diagnosis?
subject: Orthopedics
explanation: Familial short stature is most likely. The child is short in a manner consistent with parental height, grows at a normal velocity, and has bone age equal to chronological age. Such children usually enter puberty at the expected time and reach an adult height near their genetically predicted range. Constitutional delay of growth and puberty more often features delayed bone age, delayed puberty, and a family history of late maturation, with eventual catch-up. Growth-hormone deficiency typically slows growth velocity and delays bone age, often with relative preservation of weight. Undernutrition affects weight and growth and requires dietary and systemic assessment. Evaluation must plot accurate serial measurements, calculate mid-parental target height, assess puberty and body proportions, and look for red flags such as crossing centiles, systemic symptoms, dysmorphism, or disproportion before labeling a normal variant.

question: A 45-year-old smoker with hemoptysis has a 3-cm peripheral apical lung mass on staging contrast CT, with no endobronchial lesion and no safer accessible nodal or metastatic target. Which listed method is most appropriate for tissue diagnosis?
subject: Radiology
explanation: For a peripheral apical lung mass already characterized on staging CT, with no safer accessible lymph node or metastatic site, CT-guided percutaneous transthoracic biopsy generally offers higher diagnostic yield than bronchoscopy. Bronchoscopy is favoured for a central or endobronchial lesion and may be combined with endobronchial ultrasound when mediastinal nodes are the best staging target. Biopsy planning should obtain diagnosis and stage with the least invasive highest-yield procedure; PET/CT or brain imaging may identify a preferable target before sampling. CT-guided biopsy risks pneumothorax and bleeding, especially with emphysema or a long aerated-lung path. Sputum cytology has limited sensitivity for a peripheral lesion, and sputum AFB testing alone cannot establish or exclude malignancy.

question: Which abnormality is present by definition in every form of circulatory shock?
subject: Physiology
explanation: All forms of shock share inadequate effective tissue perfusion and oxygen delivery relative to metabolic demand, leading to cellular hypoxia and organ dysfunction. The hemodynamic mechanism differs: hypovolemic and cardiogenic shock often have low cardiac output and high systemic resistance, distributive shock may have low resistance and warm skin early, and obstructive shock impedes cardiac filling or outflow. Cold clammy skin and increased peripheral resistance therefore are not universal, and respiratory rate usually rises rather than falls but can vary late. Recognition relies on mental status, skin, capillary refill, urine output, lactate trends, blood pressure, pulse pressure, and the underlying cause; normal blood pressure does not exclude compensated shock.

question: A young woman with obesity has nine months of daily headache that is worse when recumbent, transient visual obscurations, and pulsatile tinnitus. Examination shows bilateral papilledema without a focal neurological deficit, and MRI with venography shows no mass lesion or cerebral venous sinus thrombosis. What is the most likely diagnosis?
subject: ObGy
explanation: The syndrome is idiopathic intracranial hypertension, historically called pseudotumor cerebri. The typical patient is a young woman with obesity who has pressure-type headache, transient visual obscurations, pulsatile tinnitus, and papilledema without a localizing deficit other than possible sixth-nerve palsy. Diagnosis requires neuroimaging, including venous imaging, to exclude a mass and cerebral venous sinus thrombosis, followed by lumbar puncture showing elevated opening pressure with otherwise normal cerebrospinal fluid. Vision is the major threatened function, so formal visual fields and urgent ophthalmic follow-up are essential. Weight reduction and acetazolamide are main treatments; oral contraceptives are not an established independent cause.

question: A patient has a recurrent complex fistula-in-ano with multiple external openings after previous surgery. Which listed investigation best maps the primary tract, secondary extensions, abscesses, and relation to the sphincter complex before further treatment?
subject: Surgery
explanation: MRI pelvis is the best listed study for mapping a recurrent complex fistula-in-ano. High-resolution multiplanar sequences show the primary tract, internal opening, relation to internal and external sphincters, secondary branches, horseshoe extensions, supralevator spread, and occult abscesses, enabling surgery that controls sepsis while preserving continence. Endoanal ultrasound can be useful, especially for sphincter assessment, but has a smaller field and may be limited by pain, stenosis, or complex supralevator disease. Fistulography poorly defines soft-tissue planes, and routine CT is less accurate for tract mapping though useful in acute sepsis or when MRI is unavailable. A simple primary fistula may be diagnosed clinically; imaging is particularly valuable in recurrent, Crohn-related, multiple, or atypical disease.

question: Which complication is the most important DKA-related cause of mortality in children?
subject: Medicine
explanation: Cerebral edema is the most important DKA-related cause of death in children, although it is uncommon. Warning signs include headache, slowing heart rate, rising blood pressure, altered behavior or consciousness, cranial-nerve abnormalities, incontinence, and oxygen desaturation; treatment with hypertonic saline or mannitol should begin immediately when suspected rather than waiting for imaging. Careful isotonic fluid, insulin timing, electrolyte monitoring, and avoidance of unnecessary rapid osmotic shifts reduce risk, while adult DKA mortality more often reflects precipitating disease and comorbidity.

question: Which of the following is not included in the Gaisböck syndrome?
subject: Medicine
explanation: Gaisbock syndrome is relative erythrocytosis from reduced plasma volume, classically described in an overweight or obese hypertensive man, often with smoking, alcohol use, stress, or diuretic exposure. Red-cell mass is not truly expanded as in polycythemia vera, and leukocyte and platelet counts are generally normal. Hypotension is therefore the feature that does not fit; evaluation must still exclude hypoxia, sleep apnea, renal or endocrine erythropoietin drive, and a clonal myeloproliferative disorder.

question: Identify the given instrument.
subject: Dermatology
explanation: The instrument has long straight jaws that place linear rows of staples across tissue, identifying a linear stapler. Depending on the model, a linear cutter may also divide tissue between staple rows and is used in bowel, lung, and other resections or anastomoses. A circular stapler has a round head for end-to-end or end-to-side anastomosis, while a skin stapler is a small external device for wound closure.

question: A patient has exertional dyspnea, elevated jugular venous pressure that rises with inspiration, a pericardial knock, and imaging showing a thickened calcified pericardium, without a valvular murmur. What is the diagnosis?
subject: Physiology
explanation: The combination of Kussmaul sign, a pericardial knock, and a thickened calcified pericardium indicates constrictive pericarditis. A rigid pericardium abruptly limits diastolic filling, producing elevated systemic venous pressure, rapid early filling followed by sudden cessation, ventricular interdependence, edema, ascites, and exertional dyspnea. Jugular venous pressure may rise with inspiration because the right ventricle cannot accommodate increased venous return. Restrictive cardiomyopathy and right-ventricular infarction can also produce Kussmaul sign, which is why the added imaging and knock matter. Tricuspid stenosis usually has a diastolic murmur; aortic regurgitation and pulmonary arterial hypertension have different pulse and auscultatory patterns.

question: Inhalational induction of choice in children is:
subject: Anesthesia
explanation: Sevoflurane is nonpungent, causes little airway irritation, and has a low blood-gas partition coefficient, allowing smooth and relatively rapid inhalational induction in children who do not have intravenous access. Desflurane is pungent and can provoke coughing, laryngospasm, and sympathetic stimulation; isoflurane is also pungent and slower. Halothane was historically popular for pediatric induction but has slower recovery and greater concerns about myocardial depression, arrhythmia, and rare hepatotoxicity.

question: A patient was brought to the emergency department following a road traffic accident. Skin grafting was done wherein the graft was taken from the same person. Which type of graft is it?
subject: Surgery
explanation: A graft transferred from one site to another in the same individual is an autograft. Split-thickness and full-thickness skin grafts used for trauma or burns are common examples; because donor and recipient are genetically identical, immune rejection does not occur, although graft failure can result from hematoma, shear, infection, poor vascular bed, or technical problems. An allograft comes from another genetically different human and is often temporary unless immunosuppression or specialized tissue circumstances apply. An isograft is exchanged between genetically identical individuals, such as monozygotic twins. A xenograft comes from another species, for example porcine skin used as a temporary biologic dressing. Successful autografting still requires meticulous bed preparation, immobilization, and postoperative care.

question: Which of the following is most suitable for intravenous regional anesthesia?
subject: Anesthesia
explanation: Lidocaine is the standard listed agent for intravenous regional anesthesia, or a Bier block, because it provides reliable anesthesia with a safer cardiovascular profile if some drug escapes the tourniquet. The limb is exsanguinated, a tourniquet isolates its circulation, and dilute preservative-free local anesthetic is injected intravenously. Bupivacaine should be avoided because accidental systemic release can cause severe, treatment-resistant cardiotoxicity; tourniquet timing and readiness to manage local-anesthetic systemic toxicity remain essential.

question: Which of the following airway devices helps maintain FiO2 of 0.25-0.60, irrespective of the patient's breathing effort?
subject: Anesthesia
explanation: A Venturi mask is a fixed-performance oxygen device that entrains a calibrated amount of room air through a color-coded jet and can deliver a relatively predictable FiO2, commonly about 0.24-0.60. Its total gas flow must exceed the patient's peak inspiratory demand for that accuracy to hold. Nasal cannulae and simple masks are variable-performance devices, while a non-rebreather can deliver a higher concentration but its actual FiO2 depends on seal, reservoir inflation, and respiratory pattern.

question: A 5-year-old child presented with recurrent respiratory infections with thickened sputum. Chest X-ray showed bronchial wall thickening. He has been suffering from steatorrhea since birth. Which of the following is the first differential diagnosis?
subject: Pediatrics
explanation: Recurrent sinopulmonary infection with thick secretions plus steatorrhea from pancreatic insufficiency strongly suggests cystic fibrosis. Pathogenic variants in CFTR impair chloride and bicarbonate transport, dehydrating secretions in airways, pancreas, intestine, reproductive tract, and sweat ducts. Diagnosis uses sweat chloride testing performed to standard after a positive newborn screen or suggestive phenotype, with CFTR genetic analysis and functional testing when results are intermediate or discordant. Management is multidisciplinary and genotype informed, including airway clearance, antibiotics, pancreatic enzymes, fat-soluble vitamins, high-energy nutrition, and CFTR modulators for eligible variants. Alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency can cause liver and later lung disease but does not explain this classic childhood combination.

question: What is the most common cause of Cushing's disease in a 60-year-old male?
subject: Pathology
explanation: Cushing disease specifically means ACTH-dependent hypercortisolism caused by a pituitary corticotroph adenoma, most often a microadenoma. Adrenal cortisol-producing tumors and ectopic ACTH secretion cause Cushing syndrome but are not called Cushing disease. Age does not alter that definition. Evaluation first establishes endogenous hypercortisolism with appropriate screening tests, such as late-night salivary cortisol, 24-hour urinary free cortisol, or an overnight dexamethasone-suppression test; plasma ACTH then separates ACTH-dependent from ACTH-independent disease. Pituitary MRI and sometimes inferior petrosal sinus sampling help localize ACTH secretion after biochemical confirmation. Small-cell lung cancer is an important ectopic ACTH source but would therefore represent ectopic Cushing syndrome.

question: A woman with bone pain, abdominal symptoms, and neuropsychiatric change has repeatedly elevated corrected calcium with an inappropriately elevated PTH, confirming primary hyperparathyroidism. Surgery is planned. Which listed imaging test best localizes a hyperfunctioning parathyroid adenoma preoperatively?
subject: Orthopedics
explanation: Technetium-99m sestamibi scintigraphy is the best listed test for preoperative localization of a hyperfunctioning parathyroid adenoma after primary hyperparathyroidism has already been established biochemically. It is often combined with cervical ultrasound, and SPECT/CT can improve anatomic localization, especially for ectopic glands or reoperative cases. Neither sestamibi nor any other imaging study diagnoses primary hyperparathyroidism: repeated corrected or ionized hypercalcemia with an inappropriately high PTH does that, after evaluating renal function, vitamin D, medications, and relevant differential diagnoses. Imaging is performed only when an operation is planned and should not be used to decide whether the biochemical disease exists. MRI, CT, and newer PET techniques have roles when first-line localization is negative or discordant. A negative localization study does not exclude disease or preclude bilateral exploration by an experienced surgeon.

question: A 50-year-old woman presented with difficulty in activities like climbing stairs, getting up from a chair, and combing hair. Violaceous erythema of the upper eyelids was noted. What is the most probable diagnosis?
subject: Dermatology
explanation: Symmetric proximal weakness affecting stair climbing, rising from a chair, and overhead activity together with a violaceous heliotrope eyelid rash indicates dermatomyositis. Other cutaneous clues include Gottron papules, shawl or V signs, periungual change, and mechanic's hands; evaluation includes muscle enzymes, myositis antibodies, imaging or electromyography, and sometimes biopsy. Adults require age-appropriate malignancy assessment, and treatment usually combines corticosteroids with a steroid-sparing immunomodulator tailored to lung, swallowing, skin, and muscle involvement.

question: A patient develops an acute isolated lower-motor-neuron facial palsy with inability to wrinkle the forehead, close the eye tightly, or elevate the mouth corner. Which facial distribution is affected in Bell palsy?
options: Ipsilateral upper and lower halves of the face
Contralateral upper and lower halves of the face
Ipsilateral lower half of the face
Contralateral lower half of the face
subject: Medicine
explanation: Bell palsy is an acute peripheral seventh-cranial-nerve palsy affecting the ipsilateral upper and lower face. The patient cannot wrinkle the forehead, close the eye tightly, or elevate the mouth corner; a supranuclear lesion usually spares the forehead because its motor nucleus has bilateral cortical input. Diagnosis requires exclusion of stroke, vesicular Ramsay Hunt syndrome, Lyme disease where relevant, otologic disease, trauma, and mass lesions, with early corticosteroid and meticulous eye protection central to treatment.

question: Which listed drug has randomized-trial evidence for reducing mortality in stable heart failure with reduced ejection fraction when started and titrated appropriately?
subject: Pharmacology
explanation: Metoprolol succinate extended release is one of the beta blockers proven to reduce mortality and hospitalization in stable heart failure with reduced ejection fraction, alongside carvedilol and bisoprolol. It is started at a low dose when the patient is euvolemic and clinically stable and titrated gradually; initiation or rapid escalation during shock or severe congestion can be harmful. Loop diuretics such as furosemide and torsemide relieve congestion but have not shown the same independent mortality benefit. Prazosin can reduce afterload but is not foundational HFrEF therapy. Modern guideline-directed care also includes an ARNI, ACE inhibitor or ARB, mineralocorticoid-receptor antagonist, and SGLT2 inhibitor as tolerated, with individualized device and comorbidity management.

question: A 49-year-old male presents with recurrent episodes of watery diarrhoea, dehydration, hypokalemia, and achlorhydria. Which of the following neuroendocrine tumour can be responsible for these symptoms?
subject: Pathology
explanation: VIPoma causes the WDHA syndrome: profuse watery diarrhea, hypokalemia, and achlorhydria or hypochlorhydria. Excess vasoactive intestinal peptide stimulates intestinal adenylate cyclase and chloride and water secretion while inhibiting gastric acid. The diarrhea is secretory, persists during fasting, and can cause severe dehydration, metabolic acidosis from bicarbonate loss, renal injury, hypercalcemia, and flushing. Most VIPomas arise in the pancreas in adults, while neural tumors can produce the syndrome in children. Diagnosis uses elevated fasting VIP during symptomatic high-volume diarrhea and imaging for localization. Initial treatment is aggressive fluid and electrolyte replacement; somatostatin analogues control secretion, and resection is pursued when feasible.

question: A family pedigree chart is given below. Identify the mode of inheritance of this condition.
subject: Medicine
explanation: The pedigree shows vertical transmission in successive generations, both sexes affected, father-to-son transmission, and affected heterozygous parents producing both affected and unaffected children, all supporting autosomal-dominant inheritance. Male-to-male transmission excludes X-linked inheritance, and the frequent affected parent-to-child pattern argues against a typical autosomal-recessive trait. Real pedigrees can be altered by reduced penetrance, variable expression, de novo variants, small family size, and uncertain paternity, but the displayed pattern is classic.

question: A woman has a greenish, frothy, malodorous vaginal discharge with vulvovaginal erythema. Saline wet mount shows motile flagellated protozoa. What is the most likely diagnosis?
subject: ObGy
explanation: Greenish frothy discharge, vulvovaginal inflammation, and motile flagellated protozoa on saline wet mount identify Trichomonas vaginalis infection. Wet-mount sensitivity is limited, so a negative smear does not exclude infection; a nucleic-acid amplification test is more sensitive where available. Treatment uses an oral nitroimidazole regimen, with simultaneous treatment of current sexual partners, abstinence until treatment is complete and symptoms resolve, and testing for other sexually transmitted infections. Bacterial vaginosis produces a thin homogeneous discharge, clue cells, and elevated pH without motile protozoa. Candidiasis causes pruritus with thick discharge and budding yeast or pseudohyphae, while chlamydial cervicitis is diagnosed by nucleic-acid testing.

question: The H&E image shows a markedly enlarged cell containing a dense basophilic intranuclear inclusion separated from the nuclear membrane by a clear halo. Which virus produces this classic owl-eye inclusion?
subject: Microbiology
explanation: The replacement H&E image shows a cytomegalic cell with a large dense basophilic intranuclear inclusion surrounded by a clear halo, the classic owl-eye appearance of cytomegalovirus. Smaller cytoplasmic inclusions may also occur. CMV can produce congenital disease and severe retinitis, colitis, esophagitis, pneumonitis, or encephalitis in immunocompromised hosts. Histology suggests tissue-invasive disease, but immunohistochemistry or molecular testing can improve confirmation in the correct clinical setting. HSV more often produces multinucleation, nuclear molding, margination, and Cowdry A inclusions; HPV produces koilocytosis; EBV is usually established by serology, nucleic-acid methods, or characteristic tumor-cell studies rather than this inclusion.

question: A 70-year-old male comes with complaints of hip pain. An X-ray of the hip shows lytic lesions and elevated serum calcium levels. Bone marrow plasma cells are 42%, and the cells seen are given in the image below. What is the most likely diagnosis?
subject: Orthopedics
explanation: The diagnosis is multiple myeloma. Clonal marrow plasma cells at 42%, lytic bone lesions, hypercalcemia, bone pain, and the plasma-cell morphology together satisfy a myeloma-defining clinical picture. Plasma cells have eccentric nuclei, coarse clock-face chromatin, basophilic cytoplasm, and a perinuclear hof. Diagnostic evaluation includes serum and urine monoclonal-protein studies, serum free light chains, blood count, renal function, calcium, albumin, beta-2 microglobulin, LDH, marrow immunophenotyping and cytogenetics, and whole-body low-dose CT, PET/CT, or MRI as appropriate. CLL, ALL, and CML have different clonal cells and clinical findings. Management depends on age, frailty, cytogenetic risk, renal function, transplant eligibility, and complications; urgent supportive issues include hypercalcemia, renal injury, infection, fractures, cord compression, and thrombosis prevention with selected therapies.

question: If a monozygotic conceptus divides 9-12 days after fertilization, what chorionicity and amnionicity are expected?
subject: ObGy
explanation: Division of a monozygotic conceptus about 9-12 days after fertilization occurs after formation of the chorion and amnion, producing monochorionic monoamniotic twins. Earlier division within roughly three days produces dichorionic diamniotic twins, while division around days 4-8 usually produces monochorionic diamniotic twins. Very late incomplete division, classically after about day 13, can produce conjoined twins. These time windows are useful embryologic approximations rather than exact clocks. Monoamniotic twins share one sac and have a major risk of cord entanglement in addition to complications of monochorionic placentation, so diagnosis by ultrasound and specialist surveillance materially affect antenatal management and delivery planning.

question: The following picture showed a lymph node biopsy of a 30-year-old male with cervical lymphadenopathy. The diagnosis is
subject: Pathology
explanation: The image shows a classic Reed-Sternberg-type cell: a very large atypical cell with bilobed or binucleate nuclei and prominent eosinophilic nucleoli producing an owl-eye appearance in a mixed inflammatory background. In the clinical setting of cervical lymphadenopathy, this supports classic Hodgkin lymphoma. The neoplastic cells commonly express CD30 and CD15, show weak PAX5, and usually lack strong pan-B-cell marker expression. Reed-Sternberg-like cells are not absolutely specific and may occur in reactive or other lymphoid conditions, so diagnosis requires nodal architecture, the full cellular background, immunophenotype, and clinical correlation rather than one cell alone. Excisional lymph-node biopsy is generally preferred when lymphoma is suspected.

question: A 40-year-old female presents with frequent headaches. Investigation reveals raised intracranial pressure and the presence of a dural-based tumor. Which of the following histological findings correlate with the diagnosis?
options: Perinuclear halos around fried-egg tumor cells
Psammoma bodies among whorled meningothelial cells
subject: Pathology
explanation: A dural-based tumor with whorled meningothelial cells and psammoma bodies is a meningioma. Psammoma bodies are concentrically laminated calcifications formed within whorls and are especially conspicuous in psammomatous variants. Most meningiomas are slow-growing extra-axial tumors derived from arachnoid cap cells; symptoms reflect compression and location rather than invasion of brain parenchyma. Fried-egg cells with delicate branching capillaries suggest oligodendroglioma. Flexner-Wintersteiner rosettes indicate retinoblastoma, while Homer-Wright rosettes occur in neuroblastoma and several primitive neuroectodermal tumors. Histologic grading depends on mitotic activity, brain invasion, specific atypical or anaplastic features, and increasingly molecular findings, not merely the presence of psammoma bodies.

question: What is the normal chronological sequence of lochia during the puerperium?
options: Lochia serosa, then lochia rubra, then lochia alba
Lochia rubra, then lochia alba, then lochia serosa
Lochia alba, then lochia rubra, then lochia serosa
Lochia rubra, then lochia serosa, then lochia alba
subject: ObGy
explanation: Normal puerperal lochia progresses from rubra to serosa to alba. Lochia rubra is initially blood-rich and red, usually predominating for the first several days. Lochia serosa then becomes pink or brown as blood decreases and leukocytes, mucus, and decidual material become more prominent. Lochia alba is later pale, yellow-white discharge rich in leukocytes and may continue for several weeks. Exact timing varies and transient increased redness can occur with activity or placental-site eschar separation. Foul odor, fever, uterine tenderness, heavy bleeding, large clots, hemodynamic symptoms, or return to persistently heavy rubra requires assessment for endometritis, retained tissue, subinvolution, or secondary postpartum hemorrhage.

question: A cancer patient undergoing radiotherapy is given a dose of 1.8-2 Gy once daily for 5 days per week for 6-8 weeks. What is this type of radiotherapy called?
subject: Pharmacology
explanation: Delivering approximately 1.8-2 Gy once daily, five days per week, over several weeks is conventional or regular fractionated external-beam radiotherapy. Fractionation permits repair of sublethal injury in normal tissue, reoxygenation and redistribution of tumor cells, and delivery of a therapeutic total dose. Hyperfractionation uses smaller fractions more than once daily, while accelerated schedules shorten overall treatment time, sometimes with multiple daily fractions. Brachytherapy places a radiation source within or close to the target rather than defining an external-beam fraction schedule. The optimal dose, fraction size, total treatment time, and concurrent systemic therapy vary by tumor, stage, intent, organ tolerance, technology, and evidence; the values in the question are a pattern, not a universal cancer regimen.

question: The child in the image has bilateral pitting edema, abdominal distension, dermatosis, and muscle wasting masked by edema. Which nutritional phenotype is present?
options: Isolated micronutrient deficiency without acute malnutrition
subject: Pediatrics
explanation: The photograph shows edematous severe acute malnutrition, traditionally called kwashiorkor: bilateral edema, abdominal distension, skin and hair change, apathy, and muscle wasting partly hidden by fluid retention. Modern understanding does not support a simple “protein deficiency versus calorie deficiency” split. Infection, oxidative stress, gut dysfunction, micronutrient deficits, metabolic adaptation, and an inadequate overall diet interact in the edematous phenotype. Marasmus describes severe wasting without nutritional edema, and mixed phenotypes occur. Treatment follows a staged severe-malnutrition protocol because aggressive fluid, sodium, protein, calories, or early iron can be dangerous. Immediate assessment includes glucose, temperature, infection, dehydration or shock, anemia, electrolytes, appetite, edema severity, and other complications.

question: Which of the following finding is shown in the given image?
subject: Medicine
explanation: The dark vertical midline pigmentation extending along the abdomen in pregnancy is linea nigra. It represents hormonally increased melanogenesis of the normally less visible linea alba and commonly becomes prominent from the pubis toward the umbilicus or xiphoid; it usually fades after delivery. Striae gravidarum are linear dermal stretch marks, while chloasma or melasma produces facial hyperpigmented patches rather than a single abdominal midline line.

question: A 5-year-old girl is brought to the clinic with a fever, cough, red eyes, running nose, and rash. The appearance of the child is shown below. What is the diagnosis?
subject: ENT
explanation: Fever, cough, coryza, conjunctivitis, and a spreading rash identify measles, and the buccal image shows Koplik spots opposite the molars. These tiny bluish-white lesions on an erythematous base often precede the cephalocaudal morbilliform eruption and are highly characteristic. Management includes airborne isolation, supportive care, public-health notification, and age-appropriate vitamin A in children according to guidance; mumps, varicella, and erythema infectiosum have different prodromes and rash morphology.

question: Which of the following is not a correct match for ABCD according to pediatric advanced life support?
subject: Anesthesia
explanation: The systematic pediatric resuscitation sequence uses airway, breathing, circulation, disability, and exposure, often summarized as ABCDE. Disability is a rapid neurologic assessment that includes consciousness, pupils, seizures, and bedside glucose when indicated; dehydration may contribute to shock but is not what the letter D denotes. Immediate life threats are treated as they are found, and the sequence is repeatedly reassessed rather than completed as a single static checklist.

question: In which listed situation should vacuum-assisted vaginal delivery generally not be used?
options: Prolonged second stage at term with all prerequisites met
Maternal exhaustion at term with a low engaged cephalic head
Fetal compromise at term with all delivery prerequisites met
Cephalic preterm fetus at thirty-two completed weeks
subject: ObGy
explanation: Vacuum extraction is generally avoided before 34 completed weeks because the preterm scalp and intracranial structures are more vulnerable to subgaleal, intraventricular, and other hemorrhagic injury. Maternal exhaustion, a prolonged second stage, or a need to expedite delivery for fetal compromise can be indications only when the cervix is fully dilated, membranes are ruptured, the head is engaged, fetal position and station are known, the pelvis is adequate, analgesia and consent are addressed, and immediate caesarean delivery is available if the attempt fails. Vacuum should also be avoided with suspected fetal bleeding or bone-demineralization disorders and when operative-vaginal prerequisites are absent.

question: A 5-year-old child presented with a history of edema of the face which later progressed to generalized edema. Urine showed massive proteinuria and light microscopy was normal. Electron microscopy showed effacement of podocyte foot processes. What is the diagnosis?
subject: Medicine
explanation: Childhood nephrotic syndrome with massive proteinuria, normal glomeruli on light microscopy, and diffuse podocyte foot-process effacement on electron microscopy is minimal change disease. Immunofluorescence is usually negative, and most children respond promptly to corticosteroids after appropriate clinical evaluation. FSGS shows segmental sclerosis in some glomeruli and has a higher steroid-resistance risk, membranous nephropathy shows capillary-wall immune deposits, and post-streptococcal disease produces a nephritic syndrome with hypercellularity and subepithelial humps.

question: Identify the segment marked in red in the image below.
subject: Medicine
explanation: The highlighted inferior anterior part of the right hepatic lobe is Couinaud segment V. The right portal vein divides the right liver into anterior and posterior sectors, and the portal plane separates superior from inferior segments: V is anterior-inferior, VIII anterior-superior, VI posterior-inferior, and VII posterior-superior. Segment IV belongs to the left functional liver near the gallbladder fossa, while segment III is left lateral-inferior; segmental anatomy guides resection and interventional planning.

question: Which listed finding, by itself in the current pregnancy, is not an indication for caesarean delivery?
options: A history of a macrosomic infant in a previous pregnancy
Placenta previa completely covering the internal cervical os
Advanced cervical carcinoma obstructing the birth canal
subject: ObGy
explanation: A previous macrosomic infant does not by itself mandate caesarean delivery in the current pregnancy. The present fetal size, diabetes status, pelvic assessment, labor progress, prior obstetric outcome, and patient preferences must be evaluated. Confirmed absolute cephalopelvic disproportion prevents safe vaginal passage. Placenta previa completely covering the internal os requires caesarean birth because cervical dilation can provoke major hemorrhage. Advanced cervical carcinoma obstructing or substantially involving the birth canal also commonly requires operative delivery, individualized with the oncology team. Suspected macrosomia has its own weight thresholds and uncertainty; even current suspected large size is not equivalent to an isolated history from a previous pregnancy.

question: Identify the rib highlighted in the X-ray.
subject: Radiology
explanation: The highlighted structure is the posterior part of the third rib. On a frontal chest radiograph, posterior ribs run more horizontally and are usually more conspicuous, whereas anterior ribs slope downward toward the sternum and can be faint. Rib counting begins at the first posterior rib beneath the clavicle and proceeds inferiorly; the red segment overlies the third posterior rib. This distinction matters when localizing pulmonary lesions, pleural abnormalities, fractures, or lines, but apparent rib level can vary with patient rotation, inspiration, projection, and congenital variants. A complete image review should count from a reliable landmark rather than infer the number from the lung zone alone, and suspicious bone findings require evaluation on appropriate dedicated views or cross-sectional imaging.
