explanation: For a patient with PCOS whose priorities are cycle control, acne, and hirsutism and who is not seeking pregnancy, a combined oral contraceptive is the preferred listed first-line pharmacologic treatment when estrogen is safe. It suppresses ovarian androgen production, raises sex-hormone-binding globulin, regularizes withdrawal bleeding, and protects the endometrium. Lifestyle measures remain foundational for every patient and can improve metabolic health even without weight loss, but they do not replace symptom-directed treatment. Metformin is favored for metabolic indications and selected cycle problems when the pill is unsuitable; clomiphene is an ovulation-induction drug, while letrozole is now generally preferred first line when pregnancy is desired.

question: A patient got pricked by a rose thorn and after that, there are linear lesions along the lymphatics. What is the most likely diagnosis?
subject: Medicine
explanation: A rose-thorn inoculation followed by nodules ascending along regional lymphatic channels is lymphocutaneous sporotrichosis, caused most often by the dimorphic Sporothrix schenckii species complex. A papule or ulcer develops at the inoculation site and additional nodules appear proximally in a sporotrichoid pattern. Culture is the usual diagnostic standard, with histopathology supportive when organisms are sparse. Chromoblastomycosis produces chronic verrucous plaques, blastomycosis more often causes pulmonary or disseminated disease with broad-based budding yeast, and mucormycosis is an invasive angioinvasive infection in susceptible hosts.

question: Last link in ACLS chain of survival is?
subject: Medicine
explanation: Recovery and rehabilitation form the final link of the contemporary cardiac-arrest Chain of Survival. The preceding links encompass recognition and emergency activation, high-quality CPR, rapid defibrillation, advanced resuscitation, and post-cardiac-arrest care. Recovery extends beyond return of spontaneous circulation and hospital discharge: survivors and caregivers may need neurologic, cognitive, physical, cardiopulmonary, psychological, and social assessment, rehabilitation, and coordinated follow-up. The recovery link was added in the 2020 AHA framework and remains explicit in the updated 2025 chain, so post-cardiac-arrest care is the penultimate rather than final link.

question: A 58-year-old male presented with weakness of the lower half of the right side of the face with drooping of angle of mouth. He is able to raise his eyebrows and close eyes on both sides. Which of the following is the likely site of the lesion?
options: Infranuclear facial nerve palsy on right side
Supranuclear facial nerve palsy on right side
Infranuclear facial nerve palsy on left side
Supranuclear facial nerve palsy on left side
subject: Anatomy
explanation: Forehead wrinkling and eye closure are preserved while the contralateral lower face is weak, indicating a supranuclear facial pathway lesion. Upper facial motor neurons receive bilateral corticobulbar input, whereas the lower facial nucleus receives predominantly contralateral cortical input. A left supranuclear lesion therefore causes right lower-facial weakness with forehead sparing. A right infranuclear lesion would weaken the entire right side, including eyebrow elevation and eye closure.

question: A 30-year-old woman has multiple painful soft vulval ulcers with ragged undermined edges and a purulent granular base that bleeds on contact, together with tender suppurative inguinal lymphadenopathy. What is the most likely diagnosis?
subject: Dermatology
explanation: Multiple painful soft genital ulcers with ragged undermined edges, a purulent friable base, and tender suppurative inguinal nodes indicate chancroid caused by Haemophilus ducreyi. Primary syphilis usually causes a single painless indurated chancre with nontender nodes, donovanosis causes a painless beefy-red ulcer without true lymphadenopathy, and lymphogranuloma venereum begins with a small often unnoticed painless lesion followed by painful buboes. Current diagnosis is often clinical and exclusionary because culture and validated molecular testing are not widely available.

question: A patient presents with a history of viral infection 2 weeks back, the patient later develops acute ascending bilaterally symmetrical lower limb flaccidity over a week followed by upper limb weakness. On examination, there is loss of spinal reflexes. What is the diagnosis?
subject: Medicine
explanation: The acute, bilateral, ascending flaccid weakness and areflexia after an infection are classic for Guillain-Barre syndrome, most commonly the acute inflammatory demyelinating polyradiculoneuropathy subtype in this option set. Weakness may progress to respiratory failure, and autonomic instability can cause dangerous blood-pressure or rhythm fluctuations, so serial respiratory measurements and monitored care are essential. Cerebrospinal fluid often develops albuminocytologic dissociation, and nerve-conduction studies support subtype classification. Patients unable to walk independently or with progression receive IV immunoglobulin or plasma exchange; corticosteroids alone are ineffective.

question: During placental examination, one smooth shiny surface shows the umbilical-cord insertion and chorionic vessels radiating beneath the amnion. Which surface is this?
subject: ObGy
explanation: The fetal placental surface is smooth and shiny because it is covered by amnion. The umbilical cord inserts on this surface, and chorionic vessels can be seen radiating beneath the membrane before branching into the villous tree. The maternal or basal surface is rough, dark red, and divided by septa into cotyledons; it should be inspected after birth for completeness because missing tissue raises concern for retained placental fragments and postpartum hemorrhage. The membranes, cord vessels, insertion, true knots, infarcts, retroplacental clot, and any accessory lobe should also be assessed. Surface appearance is an anatomical distinction, while placental function and pathology require clinical and histologic context.

question: After an interpersonal conflict, a patient develops loss of voice. Neurologic and laryngeal evaluation shows positive clinical evidence that the symptom is incompatible with recognized neurologic disease, with no external incentive or intentional production. What is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Somatic symptom disorder with predominant health anxiety
Generalized anxiety disorder with somatic tension
subject: Psychiatry
explanation: The diagnosis is functional neurological symptom disorder, also called conversion disorder, because the loss of voice is supported by positive clinical evidence of incompatibility with recognized neurologic or laryngeal disease and is not intentionally produced. Diagnosis should not rest only on normal tests or a recent stressor; clinicians should demonstrate positive signs, explain the disorder respectfully, and identify neurologic, psychiatric, trauma-related, and social comorbidity. La belle indifference describes apparent lack of concern but is neither sensitive nor specific and is not a diagnosis. Malingering requires intentional symptom production for external gain, while somatic symptom disorder centers on disproportionate thoughts and behavior around symptoms. Treatment uses education and targeted speech, physical, and psychological therapy.

question: After intravenous norepinephrine, a patient develops reflex bradycardia as arterial pressure rises. What causes the slowing of the heart rate?
subject: Medicine
explanation: Norepinephrine predominantly stimulates alpha-1 receptors, causing arteriolar and venous constriction and a rapid rise in systemic arterial pressure. Stretch of the carotid-sinus and aortic-arch baroreceptors increases afferent firing to the medulla, which increases vagal and reduces sympathetic drive to the heart. This baroreflex can slow the pulse enough to outweigh norepinephrine's direct beta-1 chronotropic effect. Central alpha-2 agonism is the mechanism of drugs such as clonidine, while the Bezold-Jarisch reflex is a different cardiopulmonary reflex associated with ventricular receptor activation, hypotension, and bradycardia.

question: The image shows an oral squamous-cell carcinoma. Which listed variable is not, by itself, an established independent prognostic factor once tumour biology and stage are assessed?
options: Duration of symptoms before clinical presentation
Anatomic subsite of the primary tumor
Pathologic TNM stage of the tumor
subject: Surgery
explanation: Symptom duration alone is not an established independent prognostic factor for oral squamous-cell carcinoma once tumour biology and stage are assessed. Patients can have aggressive disease after a short history or indolent-appearing symptoms with advanced invasion, and reported duration is vulnerable to recall and access delays. TNM stage, nodal burden and extranodal extension, depth of invasion, tumour thickness, margins, perineural or lymphovascular invasion, histologic features, and anatomical subsite materially influence prognosis and treatment. Delay still matters clinically because it can allow progression and should never be dismissed, but it is not equivalent to validated pathological risk. The candidate explanation incorrectly discussed basal-cell carcinoma; the pictured oral lesion requires biopsy and complete head-and-neck staging before prognosis is assigned.

question: A 1.5-month-old male infant is brought to the outpatient department with complaints of vomiting after feeding and failure to thrive. On examination, a firm, olive-shaped mass is palpable in the upper abdomen. What is the most likely diagnosis?
subject: Pediatrics
explanation: Progressive post-feed vomiting at 4-8 weeks with poor weight gain and a palpable epigastric or right-upper-quadrant olive indicates hypertrophic pyloric stenosis. Vomiting is classically projectile and nonbilious because obstruction is proximal to the duodenum. Prolonged losses cause hypochloremic, hypokalemic metabolic alkalosis and dehydration. Ultrasound confirms an elongated pyloric channel and thickened muscle using locally validated measurements. Resuscitation and correction of electrolytes and alkalosis precede pyloromyotomy; the condition is a medical, not immediate surgical, emergency until stabilized. Gastroesophageal reflux usually lacks an olive mass and major obstruction, while esophageal atresia presents from birth with frothing, choking, and inability to pass a tube.

question: Where does vasopressin act through V2 receptors to insert aquaporin-2 channels and increase apical water permeability?
options: Proximal-tubule epithelial cells expressing sodium-glucose transporters
Principal cells of the late distal nephron and collecting duct
Thick ascending-limb cells expressing the NKCC2 cotransporter
Alpha-intercalated cells of the cortical collecting duct
subject: Physiology
explanation: Vasopressin binds V2 receptors on principal cells of the late distal tubule and collecting duct, activates cAMP and protein kinase A, and inserts aquaporin-2 water channels into the apical membrane. Water exits basolaterally through aquaporin-3 and aquaporin-4. ADH therefore acts in both cortical and medullary collecting segments, making the original separate choices multiple-correct. It also increases inner medullary collecting-duct urea permeability, helping maintain the medullary osmotic gradient. The proximal tubule reabsorbs most filtered water constitutively but is not regulated by ADH. Thick ascending limb is water impermeable and helps dilute tubular fluid while generating the corticomedullary gradient used during antidiuresis.

question: A worker who kneels for prolonged periods has pain and swelling below the patella. MRI shows fluid in the superficial infrapatellar bursa, anterior to the distal patellar tendon and tibial tuberosity. What is the diagnosis?
subject: Orthopedics
explanation: This is superficial infrapatellar bursitis, traditionally called clergyman's knee. The decisive feature is the collection anterior to the distal patellar tendon and tibial tuberosity, below the patella. Prepatellar bursitis, or housemaid's knee, lies directly over the patella; deep infrapatellar bursitis lies between the patellar tendon and tibia. Pes anserine bursitis produces inferomedial tenderness several centimeters below the joint line rather than an anterior midline swelling. Repetitive upright kneeling places pressure on the superficial infrapatellar bursa, but occupation alone is not diagnostic. Examination must distinguish a bursal collection from joint effusion, patellar-tendon disease, abscess, and gout. Suspected infection or crystal disease warrants aspiration and appropriate studies; aseptic disease is managed with pressure avoidance, protection, and anti-inflammatory care.

question: Identify the level of lymph node of the neck indicated in the diagram?
subject: Medicine
explanation: The shaded upper deep-cervical nodal group along the upper internal jugular chain, from the skull base to the inferior border of the hyoid, is level II. It includes the jugulodigastric region and is subdivided into IIa and IIb by the spinal accessory nerve. Level Ia is the submental triangle, level Ib is the submandibular triangle, and level IV is the lower jugular chain from the cricoid level to the clavicle. Accurate level assignment matters for describing metastatic spread and planning neck dissection or radiotherapy.

question: Which gonadal peptide exerts relatively selective negative feedback on pituitary FSH secretion without directly suppressing LH to the same degree?
subject: Physiology
explanation: Inhibin is the gonadal peptide that selectively suppresses pituitary FSH more than LH. In women, granulosa cells produce inhibin B mainly during the follicular phase and inhibin A mainly from the corpus luteum and placenta; in men, Sertoli-cell inhibin B reflects spermatogenic activity. It acts at the anterior pituitary to oppose activin and reduce FSH synthesis and secretion. Estradiol, progesterone, and testosterone participate in broader hypothalamic and pituitary feedback that can affect both LH and FSH. Selective does not mean absolutely no interaction with the rest of the reproductive axis; GnRH pulse frequency, activin, follistatin, and sex steroids integrate to determine gonadotropin output.

question: Which of the following health programmes is supported by DANIDA?
subject: Microbiology
explanation: DANIDA, the Danish International Development Agency, historically supported India's National Programme for Control of Blindness through infrastructure, training, cataract services, and programme development, making blindness the classic public-health answer. The programme is now called the National Programme for Control of Blindness and Visual Impairment and its current funding and partnerships are broader, so the question should be understood as a historical agency-programme association rather than a statement of exclusive present financing. SIDA was historically associated with tuberculosis support, while malaria and HIV programmes have involved other national and international partners. Such donor associations can change and should not substitute for current programme documents in practice.

question: A 29-week preterm neonate presents with a patent ductus arteriosus (PDA), salt and pepper retinopathy, and bilateral sensorineural hearing loss (SNHL). What is the most likely congenital infection?
subject: ENT
explanation: Patent ductus arteriosus, salt-and-pepper retinopathy, and sensorineural deafness constitute congenital rubella syndrome. Infection early in pregnancy can also cause cataracts, pulmonary-artery stenosis, growth restriction, microcephaly, and developmental impairment. Congenital CMV more often causes periventricular calcification and microcephaly, toxoplasmosis classically causes hydrocephalus with diffuse intracranial calcifications and chorioretinitis, and congenital varicella produces limb hypoplasia and cicatricial skin lesions. Maternal vaccination before pregnancy is the key prevention strategy because the live attenuated vaccine is contraindicated during pregnancy; affected infants also require infection-control precautions and multidisciplinary cardiac, hearing, vision, and developmental follow-up.

question: Which organ toxicity of bleomycin can be aggravated by thoracic radiation and high inspired-oxygen exposure?
subject: Pharmacology
explanation: Bleomycin's major dose-limiting toxicity is pulmonary injury, including pneumonitis that can progress to fibrosis. Risk rises with cumulative exposure, older age, renal impairment, prior or concurrent thoracic radiation, other lung-toxic therapy, and high inspired oxygen exposure, although the precise oxygen-risk relationship varies. Bleomycin generates free radicals and tissues with low bleomycin-hydrolase activity, especially lung and skin, are vulnerable. Patients require symptom assessment and appropriate pulmonary evaluation, with discontinuation and specialist management when toxicity is suspected. Myelosuppression is relatively limited compared with many cytotoxic drugs. The liver, nervous system, and stomach are not the characteristic radiation-aggravated organ toxicity represented by the question.

question: A 28-year-old woman diagnosed with schizophrenia had been compliant with her prescribed olanzapine for several months. However, she discontinued the treatment. Which of the following is the most likely reason for her discontinuation?
subject: Psychiatry
explanation: Olanzapine commonly causes increased appetite, substantial weight gain, insulin resistance, hyperglycemia, and dyslipidemia, making metabolic burden a frequent reason for discontinuation. Discuss this risk before treatment and monitor weight or BMI, waist where appropriate, blood pressure, glucose or HbA1c, lipids, diet, activity, and smoking. Shared decisions may involve behavioral intervention, metformin in selected patients, or switching to a lower-metabolic-risk antipsychotic while protecting relapse control. Olanzapine can cause sedation and anticholinergic effects but has lower acute extrapyramidal risk than potent D2 blockers. Tardive dyskinesia, dystonia, and akathisia remain possible with antipsychotics, yet they are less characteristic than weight gain in this vignette. Abrupt unsupervised stopping risks psychotic relapse.

question: For the diagnosis of Rickettsia, an antigen of Proteus is used. What is this type of antigen called?
subject: Medicine
explanation: The Weil-Felix reaction uses heterophile antigens because antibodies generated in selected rickettsial infections can cross-react with antigens on particular Proteus strains, classically OX19, OX2, or OXK. This antigenic sharing permits agglutination but does not imply that Proteus causes the rickettsial disease. The test has limited sensitivity and specificity and should not displace validated serology, PCR, or paired-sample testing when available. A sequestered antigen is normally hidden from immune surveillance, a superantigen activates many T cells nonspecifically, and an isoantigen varies among members of one species.

question: Which anatomical drainage region of the lateral nasal wall is marked on this coronal CT image?
subject: Radiology
explanation: The marked coronal CT region is the osteomeatal complex, a functional drainage pathway in the lateral nasal wall rather than one single structure. It includes the maxillary sinus ostium, ethmoid infundibulum, hiatus semilunaris, uncinate process, ethmoid bulla, and adjacent middle meatus; the frontal recess is closely related. Obstruction can impair drainage of the maxillary, frontal, and anterior ethmoid sinuses and is therefore central to chronic-rhinosinusitis imaging and endoscopic-sinus-surgery planning. The columella is the soft-tissue partition between nostrils, the maxillary sinus is the large air cavity lateral to the nasal cavity, and the nasal valve is the narrow anterior airflow segment. CT should be reviewed systematically for anatomical variants and orbital or skull-base boundaries.

question: Which is excreted in the urine in patients with pentosuria?
subject: Medicine
explanation: Essential pentosuria is a benign inherited defect of L-xylulose reductase in the uronic-acid pathway, leading to urinary excretion of L-xylulose. Because L-xylulose is a reducing sugar, older copper-reduction urine tests may be positive even though a glucose-specific oxidase strip is negative, potentially causing confusion with diabetes mellitus. Patients are generally asymptomatic and require no treatment after the diagnosis is established. D-ribose, D-ribulose, and D-xylose are not the characteristic urinary sugar produced by this enzyme defect.

question: A suction evacuation is performed, and the products of conception reveal edematous villi, a karyotype of 46, XX, and are p57kip2 negative. What is the most likely diagnosis?
subject: Medicine
explanation: Hydropic chorionic villi, a diploid 46,XX conceptus, and absent p57KIP2 expression identify a complete hydatidiform mole. Most complete moles are androgenetic: an empty ovum is fertilized and the paternal genome duplicates, so maternally expressed p57 is absent in villous cytotrophoblast and stromal cells. Partial moles are usually diandric triploid and retain maternal p57 expression, often with fetal tissue and scalloped villi. Invasive mole and choriocarcinoma are diagnosed by invasion, metastasis, and histology rather than this evacuated-villus profile.

question: A previously healthy 25-year-old adult presents with fever, headache, confusion, nausea, and neck stiffness. CSF shows marked neutrophilic pleocytosis, very low glucose, and elevated protein. Which listed empiric antimicrobial regimen is most appropriate while cultures are pending?
subject: Pharmacology
explanation: Ceftriaxone plus vancomycin is appropriate empiric therapy for acute community-acquired bacterial meningitis in the previously healthy younger adult described. A third-generation cephalosporin covers common susceptible pneumococci, meningococci, and many gram-negative organisms, while vancomycin protects against cephalosporin-nonsusceptible pneumococci until culture and susceptibility results permit narrowing. Obtain blood cultures promptly, but do not delay antibiotics for imaging or lumbar puncture when delay would result. Dexamethasone is generally given before or with the first antimicrobial dose when pneumococcal meningitis is suspected. Ampicillin must be added when Listeria coverage is needed, particularly in older, pregnant, immunocompromised, or otherwise high-risk patients. Acyclovir is added when encephalitis remains plausible.

question: Tzanck smear shows giant multinucleated cells. Which of the following is the most likely causative organism?
subject: Microbiology
explanation: A Tzanck smear showing multinucleated giant keratinocytes with nuclear molding and margination of chromatin supports a herpesvirus cytopathic effect, and HSV is the matching option. The smear may be obtained from the base of a fresh vesicle. It cannot reliably distinguish HSV-1, HSV-2, and varicella-zoster virus and is less sensitive and specific than nucleic-acid amplification testing, so lesion PCR is preferred in current practice. HPV produces koilocytes, CMV produces cytomegalic cells with owl-eye inclusions, and EBV does not produce this vesicular Tzanck pattern. A negative smear does not exclude herpes, especially from older crusted lesions.

question: After forceps delivery, a woman develops severe perineal pain, an enlarging bluish vulvovaginal swelling, blood pressure 80/60 mm Hg, and tachycardia. Which action should not be undertaken?
options: Attempt definitive repair in the ward procedure area
Begin resuscitation and transfuse blood products as indicated
Call the senior obstetric and anaesthetic teams immediately
Transfer urgently to the operating theatre for examination and control of bleeding
subject: ObGy
explanation: This patient has a large expanding vulvovaginal hematoma with hemorrhagic shock after operative delivery. Definitive repair must not be attempted in a ward procedure area. Immediate priorities are calling senior obstetric and anaesthetic help, activating hemorrhage protocols, obtaining large-bore access, sending blood tests and cross-match, resuscitating with warmed blood products and fluids as appropriate, correcting coagulopathy, and transferring to the operating theatre. Under anesthesia, the team evacuates clot, identifies and ligates bleeding vessels, obliterates dead space, and considers packing or drain placement. Persistent or inaccessible arterial bleeding may require interventional-radiology embolization. Small, stable, nonexpanding hematomas can sometimes be observed, but shock and expansion mandate intervention.

question: The image shows an erosive lesion of the glans in a patient who also has oral lichenoid lesions and violaceous-brown pigmentation over the chin. What is the most likely unifying diagnosis?
subject: Medicine
explanation: The erosive glans lesion together with oral lichenoid disease and violaceous-brown facial pigmentation is most consistent with erosive lichen planus. Genital lichen planus may be annular, papulosquamous, hypertrophic, or painful and erosive; oral lesions may show Wickham striae or erosions, and post-inflammatory pigmentation can persist. Lichen sclerosus more typically produces porcelain-white atrophic plaques, scarring, and phimosis. Zoon balanitis causes a shiny red-orange patch without the broader lichenoid pattern, while genital psoriasis usually forms well-demarcated erythema with little scale. Persistent erosions warrant specialist review and often biopsy.

question: A child presents with recurrent nosebleeds, easy bruising, and a history of prolonged bleeding after minor injuries. Investigations indicate von Willebrand disease. Which of the following does Von Willebrand factor (vWF) primarily bind to facilitate platelet adhesion?
subject: Pediatrics
explanation: von Willebrand factor bridges exposed subendothelial collagen to the platelet GPIb-IX-V receptor complex, mediating initial platelet tethering and adhesion under high shear. It also carries and stabilizes factor VIII, so von Willebrand disease can prolong bleeding through defects in both primary hemostasis and factor VIII survival. GPIIb/IIIa binds fibrinogen and vWF during platelet aggregation after activation, while P2Y12 is an ADP receptor involved in amplification. Patients commonly have mucocutaneous bleeding, epistaxis, menorrhagia, and procedure-related bleeding. Diagnosis and treatment depend on subtype and include vWF antigen and activity, factor VIII, selected multimer or genetic testing, desmopressin responsiveness, antifibrinolytics, or vWF-containing concentrate as appropriate.

question: The image shows Wrigley short outlet forceps. In which listed setting is this instrument inappropriate?
options: Assisting delivery of the fetal head at caesarean section
Delivering the aftercoming head of a breech fetus
A low or outlet forceps delivery after prerequisites are met
A suitable low occipito-posterior forceps delivery after position is confirmed
subject: ObGy
explanation: The image shows Wrigley short outlet forceps. Their short shanks and blades make them suitable for outlet or very low cephalic delivery and for selected assistance with fetal-head delivery at caesarean section. They are inappropriate for the aftercoming head of a breech fetus, for which Piper forceps were designed with a long shank and perineal curve so the blades can be applied while the fetal body is elevated. A low occipito-posterior application may be possible only after exact position, station, rotation, pelvic adequacy, and all operative-vaginal prerequisites are established. Forceps are never a substitute for caesarean delivery in obstructed labor or an unengaged head.

question: A 17-year-old has a first episode of nephrotic syndrome with edema, proteinuria above 4 g/day, and hypoalbuminemia. Renal function and complement are normal, and there is no gross hematuria, sustained hypertension, systemic disease, infection, or drug trigger. Under a pediatric idiopathic-nephrotic-syndrome protocol, what is the initial disease-specific treatment?
subject: Medicine
explanation: An uncomplicated first episode of idiopathic nephrotic syndrome managed under a pediatric protocol is treated initially with oral glucocorticoids, because steroid response is both therapeutic and prognostically informative. Cyclophosphamide is not routine induction therapy and is reserved for selected frequently relapsing, steroid-dependent, or difficult courses after risk-benefit assessment; albumin is used only for specific complications such as severe symptomatic hypovolemia or refractory edema, not as disease-modifying treatment. At age 17, careful evaluation for secondary disease is essential, and atypical findings, steroid resistance, or local adolescent/adult practice may prompt kidney biopsy and genetic testing.

question: A 3-month-old infant has ipsilateral weakness of the mouth during crying together with incomplete eyelid closure on the same side, as shown. Which lesion best explains the findings?
options: Isolated weakness of the orbicularis oculi muscle
subject: Anatomy
explanation: The photograph shows weakness involving eyelid closure as well as asymmetry of the mouth, supporting an ipsilateral lower-motor-neuron facial palsy rather than isolated congenital hypoplasia of depressor anguli oris. The facial nerve supplies orbicularis oculi and the other muscles of facial expression. Cranial nerve III elevates the upper eyelid through levator palpebrae, and Erb palsy affects the upper trunk of the brachial plexus rather than facial movement.

question: A 45-year-old female undergoes a thyroidectomy for a benign thyroid nodule. 2-3 days post- surgery, she develops perioral tingling, muscle cramps, and a positive Chvostek sign. Which of the following is the most probable cause of these symptoms?
subject: Medicine
explanation: Perioral tingling, cramps, and Chvostek sign 2-3 days after thyroidectomy indicate hypocalcemia, usually from transient postoperative hypoparathyroidism due to gland devascularization, edema, or inadvertent removal. Low parathyroid hormone reduces renal calcium retention and calcitriol production and raises phosphate. Calcium, albumin or ionized calcium, magnesium, phosphate, PTH, ECG, and symptom severity should be assessed. Symptomatic or severe acute hypocalcemia requires monitored intravenous calcium gluconate, followed by oral calcium and active vitamin D as appropriate. Hypomagnesemia can worsen or perpetuate hypocalcemia but is not the primary finding described.

question: A child has infantile hypotonia followed by hyperphagia, obesity, short stature, developmental delay, and intellectual disability. Testing shows loss of the paternal contribution at chromosome 15q11-q13. What is the diagnosis?
subject: Pediatrics
explanation: Loss of the paternal contribution at chromosome 15q11-q13 causes Prader-Willi syndrome because imprinted genes in this region are normally expressed from the paternal allele. Mechanisms include paternal deletion, maternal uniparental disomy, or an imprinting-center defect. Infancy is characterized by hypotonia and feeding difficulty, followed later by hyperphagia, obesity risk, short stature, hypogonadism, developmental and behavioral difficulties, and characteristic features. Angelman syndrome results from loss of maternal UBE3A expression in the same region and has a different neurologic phenotype. Diagnosis begins with methylation analysis, which detects the major mechanisms, followed by tests that define the molecular cause for recurrence counseling. Management requires early multidisciplinary control of nutrition, development, endocrine issues, sleep, and behavior.

question: A neonate has the small superficial vascular lesion shown on the trunk. It is enlarging during the expected early proliferative phase but is not ulcerated and is remote from the eye, airway, nose, lip, or other function-threatening site. What is the appropriate management?
options: Immediate Biopsy due to Malignancy Risk
subject: Dermatology
explanation: The small uncomplicated superficial infantile hemangioma can be observed because early proliferation is expected and most lesions later involute spontaneously. Follow-up should document growth, ulceration, and functional risk and educate caregivers about warning signs. Oral propranolol is first-line for hemangiomas threatening vision, airway, feeding, hearing, or disfigurement, and for selected ulcerated or segmental lesions; immediate biopsy, cryotherapy, or routine excision is unnecessary for the low-risk lesion described.

question: Which listed antihypertensive is contraindicated during pregnancy because fetal renin-angiotensin-system blockade can cause fetopathy?
subject: ObGy
explanation: Enalapril is an angiotensin-converting-enzyme inhibitor and is contraindicated during pregnancy. Fetal renin-angiotensin-system blockade, particularly after the first trimester, can cause renal failure or dysgenesis, oligohydramnios, pulmonary hypoplasia, skull hypoplasia, limb contractures, growth restriction, and neonatal hypotension or death. An exposed patient should stop the drug in consultation with the treating clinician and receive pregnancy-appropriate blood-pressure management and fetal assessment; necessary antihypertensive treatment should not simply be abandoned. Labetalol and extended-release nifedipine are commonly preferred, and methyldopa remains an acceptable long-established option. ACE inhibitors may be useful postpartum, including selected breastfeeding settings, but pregnancy and postpartum recommendations differ.

question: A patient involved in a road traffic accident (RTA) is brought to the Emergency Department. On examination, he is confused, localizes to pain, and opens his eyes in response to speech. What is the correct Glasgow Coma Scale (GCS) score for this patient?
subject: Surgery
explanation: The Glasgow Coma Scale score is 12: eye opening to speech is E3, confused conversation is V4, and localization of pain is M5. The score should be recorded as components, E3 V4 M5, rather than only a total because different neurologic patterns can produce the same number. Assessment must account for intubation, sedation, language, hearing, facial or limb injury, intoxication, aphasia, and pre-existing disability. Serial change is more informative than one isolated score. A GCS of 8 or less is often associated with need for airway protection, but airway decisions depend on oxygenation, ventilation, reflexes, trajectory, and injury. Pupils, focal deficits, glucose, temperature, and imaging complement GCS; it does not replace a full trauma and neurologic assessment.

question: A 45-year-old woman presents with tingling and sensory loss over the palmar aspect of the lateral three-and-a-half digits. Which nerve is most likely involved?
subject: Anatomy
explanation: The median nerve supplies palmar sensation over the lateral three-and-a-half digits and the corresponding nail beds. Tingling in this distribution is typical of median neuropathy, commonly carpal tunnel syndrome when thenar eminence sensation is spared because the palmar cutaneous branch arises proximal to the tunnel. The anterior interosseous nerve is a purely motor median branch; radial and ulnar sensory territories cover the dorsolateral and medial hand, respectively.

question: A 32-year-old female presents with complaints of fatigue, paleness, and dizziness. Laboratory investigations reveal low serum iron levels and an increased total iron-binding capacity (TIBC). Based on these findings and the presence of a negative iron balance, which of the following findings is NOT consistent with her iron metabolism status?
subject: Biochemistry
explanation: Negative iron balance leading to iron deficiency produces low serum iron and depleted stores, so ferritin should fall rather than rise in uncomplicated deficiency. Hepatic transferrin synthesis increases, raising total iron-binding capacity, while transferrin saturation becomes low because little circulating iron occupies the expanded binding capacity. Ferritin is also an acute-phase reactant and can be normal or elevated with inflammation despite iron-restricted erythropoiesis, but the stem's isolated negative-balance pattern makes increased ferritin the inconsistent finding.

question: Which hormone is detected by a standard home urine pregnancy test using antibodies directed at hCG-specific, usually beta-subunit, epitopes?
subject: ObGy
explanation: Home urine pregnancy tests detect human chorionic gonadotropin using immunochromatographic antibodies directed at epitopes that distinguish hCG, commonly associated with its beta subunit. Syncytiotrophoblast produces hCG after implantation, and urine concentration rises rapidly in early pregnancy. A positive test indicates hCG exposure but does not establish gestational age, viability, or intrauterine location; fertility medication containing hCG and rare tumors can also produce a positive result. False negatives occur when testing is too early, urine is dilute, instructions are not followed, or, rarely, very high antigen causes a hook effect. Concerning pain or bleeding needs clinical assessment regardless of a home-test result.

question: In the case of the death of a married woman within 3 years of marriage with a recent history of dowry demand, the inquest is to be conducted by?
subject: Forensic medicine & toxicology
explanation: A suspicious death of a married woman within seven years of marriage requires a magistrate inquiry, making Magistrate the correct option. Under current BNSS section 196, the nearest Magistrate empowered to hold inquests must inquire into suicide or suspicious death in the specified seven-year period, alongside the police process and postmortem requirements of section 194. The doctor performs the medicolegal autopsy and documents evidence but does not conduct the statutory inquest; a lawyer is not the inquest authority.

question: A baby presents with characteristic perioral and perianal rash, as shown, along with a history of diarrhea. Which mineral deficiency is most likely responsible?
subject: Dermatology
explanation: Periorificial and perianal dermatitis with diarrhea in an infant strongly suggests zinc deficiency, classically acrodermatitis enteropathica from impaired intestinal zinc uptake due to SLC39A4 or ZIP4 dysfunction. Acral lesions, alopecia, poor growth, irritability, and recurrent infection may accompany it, while acquired deficiency occurs with malnutrition, malabsorption, or excessive losses. Clinical response to oral zinc is often rapid, and lifelong supplementation is required for the inherited disorder with dose monitoring.

question: A 25-year-old male presents with fever, chills, and malaise, and is diagnosed with malaria caused by Plasmodium vivax. He has a history of hemolytic anemia among males in his family. Before initiating treatment, which of the following should be checked to ensure the safety of antimalarial therapy?
subject: Microbiology
explanation: Radical cure of Plasmodium vivax requires an 8-aminoquinoline, usually primaquine and in selected settings tafenoquine, to eradicate dormant liver hypnozoites. These drugs can cause dose-dependent oxidative hemolysis in people with G6PD deficiency, an X-linked condition suggested by affected male relatives. Quantitative G6PD testing should therefore precede treatment, and the chosen regimen must follow the measured activity, age, pregnancy or lactation status, and national guidance. A normal CBC or reticulocyte count does not establish enzyme safety, and liver tests do not predict oxidant hemolysis. Acute blood-stage infection still requires an effective schizonticide according to local resistance and species policy.

question: A single Widal test shows a low S. Typhi O titer (1:20), an isolated high S. Typhi H titer (1:380), and low Paratyphi A and B H titers (1:20 each). In the traditional exam interpretation, which state best fits this isolated H-antibody pattern?
options: Typical response after TAB vaccination involving all H antigens
Coinfection with S. Paratyphi A and B
Convalescent or past S. Typhi infection
subject: Microbiology
explanation: In the traditional Widal interpretation, O antibody rises earlier and falls sooner, so a meaningful rise supports recent active infection; H antibody appears later and can remain elevated during convalescence or after past exposure. An isolated high S. Typhi H titer with low O, AH, and BH titers therefore best fits convalescent or past S. Typhi infection. TAB vaccination would more typically raise H antibodies to Typhi and both Paratyphi organisms rather than only TH. Crucially, a single Widal test is unreliable in endemic settings because baseline titers, vaccination, cross-reaction, prior antibiotics, and timing vary. Blood culture before antibiotics is preferred for acute enteric-fever diagnosis.

question: A forensic expert receives a summons from a District Court to record evidence in a criminal case. However, two days earlier, they had already received a summons for a civil case scheduled on the same date. What should the doctor do?
options: Inform both courts and wait for instructions
Attend the court that issued the earlier summons
Attend the court for the criminal case
subject: Forensic medicine & toxicology
explanation: When criminal and civil summonses conflict on the same date, attendance in the criminal court takes precedence, while the doctor should promptly inform the civil court, provide the competing summons, and request directions or an adjournment. A witness should never simply ignore either process or privately choose based only on which document arrived first. Clear early communication, preservation of records, and compliance with any revised judicial direction reduce disruption and the risk of coercive steps for nonappearance.

question: A patient presents with arthritis, and during arthroscopy, blackening of the joint is noted. Ferric chloride test is positive. Which of the following enzymes is deficient, leading to this condition?
subject: Orthopedics
explanation: The deficient enzyme is homogentisate 1,2-dioxygenase, also called homogentisic acid oxidase. Its deficiency causes alkaptonuria, with accumulation and urinary excretion of homogentisic acid. Urine darkens on standing or alkalinization, and oxidized pigment deposits in connective tissue produce ochronosis of cartilage, sclera, and ear. Progressive pigmentation and degeneration of intervertebral discs and large joints cause early arthropathy; blackened cartilage at arthroscopy is characteristic. Ferric chloride can produce a transient color reaction but quantitative urinary homogentisic acid confirms the biochemical abnormality. Branched-chain alpha-ketoacid dehydrogenase deficiency causes maple-syrup urine disease, tyrosinase deficiency causes oculocutaneous albinism, and fumarase deficiency causes severe neurologic and metabolic disease. Treatment includes pain and joint care, monitoring cardiac and renal complications, and specialist consideration of nitisinone, which reduces homogentisic acid production.

question: A 2-year-old child is brought to the emergency room with acute respiratory distress following a choking episode. On auscultation, a distinct audible thud is heard over the chest with each inspiration and expiration. Where is the aspirated foreign body most likely lodged?
subject: Pediatrics
explanation: A freely mobile tracheal foreign body can produce an audible slap and a palpable thud as it moves with respiration, so the trachea is the best location. Symptoms can include sudden cough, stridor, wheeze, voice change, cyanosis, or intermittent airway obstruction after choking. A laryngeal object more often causes severe stridor, aphonia, and immediate upper-airway compromise, while an esophageal object produces dysphagia, drooling, and feeding difficulty but can secondarily compress the airway. A symptomatic airway foreign body requires urgent skilled airway management and rigid bronchoscopy for removal; blind finger sweeps are avoided. Normal radiography does not exclude a radiolucent object, so a convincing history and examination can justify bronchoscopy.

question: A patient cannot perform rapid alternating movements smoothly and loses rhythm while rapidly pronating and supinating the hands. What is this examination finding called?
subject: Medicine
explanation: Inability to perform rapid alternating movements smoothly is dysdiadochokinesia, a cerebellar coordination sign. It is tested by rapidly pronating and supinating the hands, tapping fingers, or alternating foot movements; the affected patient loses speed, rhythm, range, and sequencing. Lesions of the ipsilateral cerebellar hemisphere and its connections commonly produce the sign, often with intention tremor, dysmetria, rebound, and gait or limb ataxia. Ataxia is the broader syndrome, tremor describes rhythmic oscillation, and bradykinesia is slowness with decrement, classically associated with parkinsonism.

question: The CTG shows gradual, symmetrical fetal-heart-rate decelerations whose onset and recovery mirror each uterine contraction, with the nadir near the contraction peak. What pattern is this?
subject: ObGy
explanation: The tracing shows early decelerations: gradual, smooth, usually symmetrical fetal-heart-rate decreases that mirror uterine contractions, with onset near contraction onset, nadir near the contraction peak, and recovery by the end. They are caused by fetal-head compression and a vagal response, are common in advanced labor, and usually do not indicate hypoxemia when baseline, variability, and the rest of the trace are reassuring. Late decelerations begin and recover after the contraction and suggest uteroplacental insufficiency. Variable decelerations have abrupt, variable timing and shape from cord compression. Interpretation must integrate baseline rate, variability, accelerations, contraction frequency, clinical context, and evolution over time.

question: Which of the following antidiabetic drugs is preferred in a patient with heart and kidney disease?
subject: Biochemistry
explanation: For type 2 diabetes with heart failure and chronic kidney disease, an SGLT2 inhibitor with demonstrated outcome benefit is preferred among these classes because it reduces heart-failure hospitalization and slows kidney-disease progression beyond its glucose-lowering effect. Current ADA guidance recommends initiation for diabetic CKD at an eGFR of at least 20 mL/min/1.73 m2 when appropriate, with continuation as tolerated until kidney failure. Risks include genital mycosis, volume depletion, and euglycemic ketoacidosis; therapy is individualized, and thiazolidinediones can worsen fluid retention.

question: A renal biopsy of a patient is shown below. Identify the cells marked as ‘X’ in the given histological image.
subject: Pathology
explanation: The marked cells are lacis cells, also called extraglomerular mesangial or Goormaghtigh cells. They occupy the triangular vascular-pole region between the macula densa and the afferent and efferent arterioles and form part of the juxtaglomerular apparatus. They are continuous with intraglomerular mesangial cells and participate in signaling between tubular sodium sensing and renin-producing granular cells. Macula-densa cells are tightly packed epithelial cells within the wall of the distal tubule, whereas juxtaglomerular cells are modified smooth-muscle cells mainly in the afferent arteriolar wall. Podocytes cover glomerular capillaries on the urinary side and form filtration slits. The arrow location outside the tubular epithelium, at the vascular pole, is therefore decisive.

question: Which catheter is used for irrigation of blood clots after TURP?
subject: Medicine
explanation: After transurethral resection of the prostate, a large-bore three-way Foley catheter is used when continuous bladder irrigation is required. One lumen drains urine, blood, and irrigant; one inflates the retention balloon; and the third permits continuous inflow of irrigation fluid. The large caliber improves clot evacuation and allows manual irrigation if blockage occurs, with exact size selected according to the procedure and surgeon. A two-way catheter lacks a dedicated irrigation channel, a ureteric catheter is not designed for bladder clot management, and a Malecot catheter is a suprapubic drainage device.

question: In 2020, a retired father and his wife move into the household of their adult son and his family. Which family-life-cycle transition does this represent?
subject: PSM
explanation: When retired parents move into the household of their adult child, the family undergoes extension because additional relatives join the existing nuclear unit and create an extended family. Formation refers to establishment of a new family unit, commonly through partnership or marriage; contraction occurs when members leave, such as children establishing separate households; dissolution follows death, separation, or breakdown of the unit depending on the framework. Family-life-cycle terminology is descriptive, not a judgment about the arrangement. Clinicians should assess changes in caregiving, privacy, finances, authority, chronic-disease support, elder safety, and caregiver strain because the same transition may provide resilience while also creating conflict or burden.

question: Under the original IDSP framework, which listed pair belonged to sentinel surveillance for blood-borne or sexually transmitted infections?
subject: PSM
explanation: Under the original IDSP framework, HIV and hepatitis B belonged to the sentinel-surveillance grouping for blood-borne or sexually transmitted infections, with hepatitis C also included in the broader category. Sentinel sites collect high-quality data from selected populations and locations to track trends rather than enumerate every case. Contemporary HIV sentinel surveillance is principally organized through NACO, while IDSP and the Integrated Health Information Platform focus on epidemic-prone disease reporting and outbreak intelligence. Measles, diphtheria, typhoid, and tuberculosis have different regular-surveillance pathways. This answer is explicitly historical because programme architecture, reporting forms, and digital platforms evolve; current operational questions should use the latest official manuals.

question: A 6-month-old infant is admitted to the ICU with a history of recurrent infections, including sinusitis and otitis media, primarily caused by Staphylococcus aureus. Laboratory investigations reveal markedly decreased levels of serum immunoglobulins (IgA, IgG, IgM, and IgE) along with absent or significantly reduced plasma B cells. What is the most likely diagnosis?
subject: Medicine
explanation: The male infant develops recurrent bacterial respiratory infections after maternally transferred IgG wanes, with profound reduction of all immunoglobulin classes and absent or markedly reduced mature B cells. This is X-linked agammaglobulinemia, or Bruton disease, caused by pathogenic BTK variants that arrest B-cell maturation at the pre-B stage. Tonsils and lymph nodes may be small or absent, and enteroviral or Giardia infection can occur. Treatment is lifelong immunoglobulin replacement plus prompt antimicrobial therapy; live vaccines are avoided. DiGeorge syndrome primarily impairs T cells, CGD impairs phagocyte oxidative killing, and ataxia-telangiectasia has a combined but different phenotype.

question: A patient took acetazolamide due to high-altitude sickness. At what part of the nephron does acetazolamide act on?
options: B - thin descending limb of the loop of Henle
subject: Pharmacology
explanation: Acetazolamide acts predominantly in the proximal convoluted tubule, label A in the diagram, where it inhibits luminal and intracellular carbonic anhydrase. This reduces hydrogen-ion secretion and bicarbonate reclamation, causing bicarbonaturia, alkaline urine, mild natriuresis, and a hyperchloremic metabolic acidosis. The induced acidosis stimulates ventilation and helps acclimatization at altitude; other uses include glaucoma, idiopathic intracranial hypertension, and selected metabolic alkalosis. It is a relatively weak diuretic because downstream nephron segments reclaim much of the delivered sodium. Important adverse effects include paresthesia, hypokalemia, volume depletion, calcium-phosphate stones, and worsening acidosis. It should not be mistaken for loop, thiazide, or collecting-duct diuretics.

question: Which electrolyte abnormality will lead to cardiac arrhythmia in a patient with severe vomiting?
subject: Medicine
explanation: Repeated vomiting causes chloride and volume loss, metabolic alkalosis, secondary aldosterone activation, and increased renal potassium wasting; alkalemia also shifts potassium into cells. Hypokalemia therefore becomes the electrolyte disturbance most directly associated with ventricular ectopy and potentially dangerous arrhythmias in this setting. ECG findings include flattened or inverted T waves, ST depression, prominent U waves, and an apparent prolonged QU interval. Treatment requires attention to volume and chloride depletion, potassium replacement with monitoring, magnesium correction when low, and control of the cause; rapid unmonitored potassium administration is hazardous.

question: A patient has diffuse abdominal pain, generalized tenderness, and board-like rigidity. The erect chest radiograph shows free gas beneath the diaphragm. What is the most likely clinical diagnosis?
subject: Radiology
explanation: Free intraperitoneal gas beneath the diaphragm on an erect radiograph, together with sudden diffuse pain, generalized tenderness, and board-like rigidity, indicates perforation peritonitis until proved otherwise. A perforated peptic ulcer is one possible cause, but the clinical diagnosis is the resulting hollow-viscus perforation with peritoneal contamination, not uncomplicated ulcer disease. Immediate care includes resuscitation, analgesia, antibiotics with gram-negative and anaerobic coverage, laboratory assessment, surgical consultation, and source control; unstable patients should not be delayed for unnecessary imaging. CT is more sensitive and can localize the perforation when the patient is stable. Pancreatitis can cause ileus and pain but does not usually create free subdiaphragmatic gas unless a separate perforation or intervention has occurred.

question: A previously unvaccinated person living with HIV sustains multiple transdermal dog bites, a WHO category III rabies exposure. What post-exposure prophylaxis is required?
options: Immediate wound washing, immunoglobulin infiltration, and a complete vaccine course
Immediate wound washing without immunoglobulin or vaccine
Immediate wound washing and vaccine without immunoglobulin
Immediate wound washing and immunoglobulin without vaccine
subject: Microbiology
explanation: Multiple transdermal bites are WHO category III exposure. A previously unvaccinated patient requires immediate vigorous wound washing with soap and running water for about 15 minutes, a virucidal agent when available, prompt rabies vaccine, and rabies immunoglobulin infiltrated into and around every wound as anatomically feasible, with any remainder handled according to current product guidance. HIV does not remove any component; immunocompromised patients require a complete intramuscular regimen and serologic confirmation of response where recommended. RIG is omitted only in previously vaccinated people. Because rabies is nearly uniformly fatal after symptoms begin, prophylaxis is urgent and observation of an animal must follow local public-health rules.

question: Which metabolic byproduct is most directly implicated in the pathogenesis of diabetic cataracts?
subject: Medicine
explanation: In the diabetic lens, aldose reductase reduces excess glucose to sorbitol. Because lens fibers have limited capacity to metabolize sorbitol further, it accumulates, draws water osmotically, alters redox balance, and contributes to protein and fiber injury that promotes cataract formation. The same polyol-pathway concept contributes to diabetic injury in other tissues, although chronic glycation and oxidative stress also matter. Galactitol is the analogous accumulated polyol in galactosemia, where aldose reductase converts galactose to galactitol; mannitol and fructose are not the characteristic trapped products in diabetic cataract.

question: A woman has chronic nasal obstruction, roominess of the nasal cavities, greenish crusts, and anosmia despite a foul odor noticed by others. What is the most likely diagnosis?
subject: ENT
explanation: A roomy nasal cavity containing thick greenish crusts, with obstruction and inability to perceive the offensive odor noticed by others, is primary atrophic rhinitis or ozena. Progressive mucosal and turbinate atrophy impairs humidification and mucociliary clearance; the apparent obstruction comes from crusting despite the widened cavity. Allergic rhinitis causes pale edematous mucosa and watery symptoms, rhinitis sicca is localized dryness, and rhinoscleroma is a chronic granulomatous infection with infiltrative stages rather than diffuse turbinate atrophy.

question: After recurrent vomiting, an ABG shows alkalemia with elevated bicarbonate and an appropriately increased PaCO2. What is the primary acid-base disorder?
subject: Physiology
explanation: Recurrent vomiting removes gastric hydrochloric acid and chloride, producing a primary metabolic alkalosis with elevated bicarbonate and alkalemia. Respiratory compensation is hypoventilation, which raises PaCO2; oxygenation limits how far this compensation can proceed. Volume contraction, chloride depletion, hypokalemia, secondary aldosterone, and reduced renal bicarbonate excretion maintain the alkalosis. An appropriately elevated PaCO2 therefore does not make respiratory acidosis the primary disorder. Treatment addresses the cause, volume and chloride depletion, potassium, and ongoing gastric loss; chloride-responsive alkalosis often improves with isotonic saline and potassium chloride when clinically appropriate. Severe or atypical values require checking for mixed disorders and renal or endocrine causes.

question: A patient with HIV presents with bouts of cough and fever, and tuberculosis is suspected. Which culture medium is appropriate for diagnosis?
subject: Microbiology
explanation: Lowenstein-Jensen medium is an egg-based selective solid medium used to culture Mycobacterium tuberculosis complex; malachite green suppresses contaminants, and rough buff colonies appear only after prolonged incubation. Liquid culture systems such as MGIT detect growth faster and support drug-susceptibility testing. In current tuberculosis programmes, a rapid molecular test is generally performed early because it detects M. tuberculosis and key resistance markers much sooner than culture, especially important in people living with HIV. Culture remains essential for comprehensive susceptibility and difficult diagnoses. Tinsdale and Loeffler media are associated with Corynebacterium diphtheriae, while MacConkey agar selects enteric Gram-negative bacilli rather than mycobacteria.

question: A young adult with chronic hemolytic anemia and sickled erythrocytes has had multiple hospital admissions for fever, chest pain, hypoxemia, and new pulmonary infiltrates. Which underlying disorder best explains these recurrent acute-chest episodes?
subject: Pathology
explanation: Recurrent acute chest syndrome in a patient with chronic hemolysis and sickled erythrocytes indicates sickle cell disease. Acute chest syndrome is defined by a new pulmonary infiltrate together with compatible symptoms or signs such as fever, chest pain, cough, tachypnea, wheeze, or hypoxemia. Infection, pulmonary infarction from sickling, and fat embolism can overlap, and rapid progression can be fatal. Management includes oxygen when hypoxemic, analgesia, careful hydration, incentive spirometry, antibiotics with appropriate coverage, and simple or exchange transfusion according to severity. Beta-thalassemia major causes severe microcytic anemia but not sickling. Hemoglobin D-Punjab trait is usually asymptomatic, although compound states with HbS can produce sickling disease and require hemoglobin analysis.

question: An adult has a moderate-to-severe acute migraine with unilateral throbbing pain, photophobia, nausea, and vomiting. An adequate dose of a simple analgesic has not helped, and there is no pregnancy, uncontrolled hypertension, ischemic vascular disease, or hemiplegic/brainstem aura. Which listed treatment is most appropriate for this attack?
subject: Medicine
explanation: For a moderate-to-severe migraine that has not responded to an adequate simple analgesic, sumatriptan is an appropriate attack-specific treatment when contraindications are absent. As a 5-HT1B/1D agonist it reduces trigeminal neuropeptide release and cranial nociceptive transmission; route is selected according to vomiting, desired speed, and patient preference. Acetaminophen with caffeine can be reasonable for a milder early attack but has already been surpassed by the stated clinical context. Propranolol and topiramate are preventive therapies, not rescue drugs. Triptans are avoided in important ischemic vascular disease, uncontrolled hypertension, and selected aura syndromes.

question: After a fall, a 23-year-old man has severe shoulder pain, loss of the rounded deltoid contour, and the arm held slightly abducted and externally rotated. The humeral head is palpable anteriorly. What is the diagnosis?
subject: Orthopedics
explanation: The findings indicate an anterior shoulder dislocation, usually a subcoracoid displacement. The arm is held in slight abduction and external rotation, the normal rounded deltoid contour is lost, and the humeral head may be palpable anteriorly. Posterior dislocation is associated with seizures or electric shock but typically fixes the arm in adduction and internal rotation and can be missed on an AP view. Inferior dislocation, or luxatio erecta, presents with the arm locked in marked overhead abduction. A Bankart lesion is an anteroinferior labral injury associated with anterior instability, not the dislocation position itself. Before and after prompt reduction, document axillary-nerve sensation and deltoid function plus distal perfusion. Post-reduction radiographs confirm alignment and assess fractures; rehabilitation and recurrent-instability evaluation depend on age, mechanism, bone loss, and activity.

question: During which stage of witness examination are leading questions ordinarily permitted?
subject: Forensic medicine & toxicology
explanation: Leading questions ordinarily may be asked during cross-examination because the adverse party is testing the witness's accuracy, credibility, and account. Under section 146 of the Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam, a leading question suggests the desired answer; it is generally barred on objection during examination-in-chief or re-examination unless the court permits it, while introductory or undisputed matters may be led. A dying declaration is a type of relevant statement, not a stage of courtroom examination.

question: A patient develops sudden severe dyspnea and hypotension with unilateral absent breath sounds, hyperresonance, distended neck veins, and tracheal deviation away from the affected side. What is the most likely diagnosis?
subject: Radiology
explanation: Hypotension, unilateral absent breath sounds, hyperresonance, distended neck veins, and contralateral tracheal deviation identify tension pneumothorax. Increasing pleural pressure collapses the ipsilateral lung, shifts the mediastinum, reduces venous return, and causes obstructive shock. This is a clinical diagnosis: immediate decompression should not wait for a chest radiograph when the patient is unstable. Current trauma practice commonly uses finger thoracostomy or appropriately sited large-bore needle decompression according to local protocol, followed by definitive tube thoracostomy. Hemothorax causes dullness rather than hyperresonance; pulmonary contusion produces parenchymal opacity and hypoxemia without the same pressure physiology; traumatic simple pneumothorax lacks tension physiology. Reassessment is essential because decompression can fail or catheters can kink or miss the pleural space.

question: Identify the “?” marked area in the given image.
subject: Medicine
explanation: The marked peritoneal recess lies between the posterior uterus and anterior rectum and is the rectouterine pouch, or pouch of Douglas. In an upright woman it is the most dependent intraperitoneal pelvic space, so blood, pus, ascites, or malignant fluid may collect there and can sometimes be sampled through the posterior vaginal fornix. The vesicouterine pouch lies anteriorly between bladder and uterus, Morrison pouch is the right hepatorenal recess in the upper abdomen, and the ischioanal fossa is an extraperitoneal fat-filled space lateral to the anal canal.

question: Identify the orthopedic traction splint shown in the image.
subject: Orthopedics
explanation: The instrument is a Bohler-Braun splint, a metal frame with pulleys that supports the leg while providing balanced suspension and longitudinal traction. It was traditionally used for femoral-shaft, supracondylar, and selected tibial fractures, allowing adjustment of traction direction while the limb is supported. A Thomas splint has a proximal ring and side bars and is widely used for temporary immobilization and traction in femoral fractures. Buck traction is skin traction applied through straps or a boot and weights rather than the illustrated multi-pulley frame. The term Volkmann splint is not the appropriate identification here. Modern definitive fracture care has reduced prolonged balanced traction because of immobility complications, but the device remains an important examination and historical management concept. Correct setup requires line-of-pull checks, pressure-area care, neurovascular monitoring, and prevention of equinus.

question: Which cardiovascular examination finding is not considered a normal flow-related change of pregnancy and requires evaluation for structural heart disease?
subject: ObGy
explanation: A diastolic murmur is not a normal physiologic finding of pregnancy and should be evaluated for structural heart disease, such as mitral or aortic regurgitation or stenosis depending on its timing and location. Pregnancy increases plasma volume, stroke volume, heart rate, and cardiac output, so a soft midsystolic flow murmur, louder first heart sound, and physiologic third heart sound can occur without disease. Intensity, radiation, symptoms, cyanosis, abnormal second sound, persistent tachycardia, elevated jugular venous pressure, or signs of failure increase concern. Echocardiography is safe in pregnancy and is appropriate when examination or clinical history suggests pathology.

question: Which of the following enzymes depends on vitamin C for its activity?
subject: Biochemistry
explanation: Prolyl hydroxylase requires vitamin C to maintain active-site iron in the reduced Fe2+ state while hydroxylating proline residues in procollagen, a step needed for stable triple-helix formation. Lysyl hydroxylase is also ascorbate dependent, but it was not present in the original source option set; the website's prior wording had incorrectly substituted it for another choice. Lysyl oxidase instead requires copper for extracellular cross-linking, while the other named enzymes are not the relevant ascorbate-dependent collagen hydroxylase.

question: What is the mechanism of action of N-acetylcysteine in paracetamol toxicity?
options: Restores hepatic glutathione to detoxify NAPQI
Enhances renal excretion of unchanged paracetamol
Irreversibly binds paracetamol in the gastrointestinal tract
subject: Pharmacology
explanation: N-acetylcysteine supplies cysteine for hepatic glutathione synthesis and can also directly reduce the reactive acetaminophen metabolite NAPQI. In therapeutic dosing, most paracetamol undergoes glucuronidation or sulfation; a small CYP-mediated fraction becomes NAPQI and is detoxified by glutathione. In overdose, conjugation pathways saturate, glutathione is depleted, and NAPQI binds hepatocellular proteins. Treatment is most effective when started within 8 hours, but it remains indicated later when the nomogram, timing, concentration, liver injury, or clinical circumstances warrant it. Activated charcoal may reduce absorption after a recent substantial ingestion, but it does not replace N-acetylcysteine. Serial liver tests, INR, glucose, renal function, and acid-base status guide care.

question: A 30-year-old man falls astride a rigid beam and develops inability to void, blood at the urethral meatus, and a butterfly-pattern perineal hematoma. Which urethral segment is most likely injured?
subject: Surgery
explanation: A straddle injury compresses the anterior urethra against the pubic arch, most commonly injuring the bulbar urethra. Blood at the meatus, inability to void, and a butterfly perineal hematoma are classic clues. A pelvic-fracture urethral injury more often affects the membranous or prostatomembranous posterior urethra and may accompany a high-riding prostate, although examination findings are unreliable. A urethral catheter should not be passed blindly when injury is suspected; retrograde urethrography defines the site before instrumentation in a stable patient, and urinary drainage is established according to injury pattern and expertise. Posterior urethral valves are congenital, while intraperitoneal bladder rupture causes intraperitoneal urinary leakage and follows a different diagnostic cystography pathway.

question: Which of the following is not a food adulteration disease?
subject: Medicine
explanation: Fluorosis is not classically a food-adulteration illness; it results from chronic excessive fluoride exposure, most importantly through drinking water in endemic regions, and causes dental mottling and later skeletal disease. Epidemic dropsy follows contamination of edible oil, especially mustard oil, with Argemone oil. Neurolathyrism follows heavy consumption of Lathyrus sativus containing the excitatory neurotoxin beta-ODAP and has historically been linked to pulse substitution or adulteration. Aflatoxicosis results from fungal contamination of stored food. Modern public-health terminology distinguishes deliberate adulteration from accidental or biologic contamination, but fluorosis remains the clear exception.

question: For a clinically stable term newborn who remains in hospital, when is the preferred heel-prick dried-blood-spot sample collected for congenital-hypothyroidism screening?
options: Umbilical-cord blood is the only acceptable sample
Within the first 12 hours after birth
subject: Pediatrics
explanation: For a stable term infant, Indian congenital-hypothyroidism screening guidance prefers a heel-prick dried-blood-spot sample at 48-72 hours of age. This interval allows the immediate physiologic postnatal TSH surge to subside while preserving early detection. Current AAP guidance allows collection after 24 hours, preferably 48-72 hours, and before discharge; a specimen obtained early is preferable to a missed screen but may require repeat testing. Cord blood is used in some programs but is not the only acceptable specimen. Abnormal screening requires prompt confirmatory serum free T4 and TSH and timely levothyroxine when indicated. Preterm, ill, transfused, or very-low-birth-weight infants often need repeat screening because delayed TSH elevation or sampling effects can cause false reassurance.

question: In the traditional health-education model, what is the correct sequence by which a person adopts a new health practice?
subject: Medicine
explanation: The traditional health-education adoption sequence is awareness, interest, evaluation, trial, and adoption. A person first learns that the practice exists, seeks further information, judges its relevance and expected benefit, tries it on a limited basis, and then incorporates it more consistently. Real behavior change is not always linear: cost, access, social norms, self-efficacy, reinforcement, relapse, and structural barriers can interrupt or reverse the sequence. Nevertheless, among the listed ordered models, trial belongs after evaluation and before adoption; omitting it makes the older teaching sequence incomplete.

question: In a patient with a positive pregnancy test, pain, and vaginal bleeding, which transvaginal-ultrasound finding most strongly suggests a tubal ectopic pregnancy?
options: Thin endometrium and no adnexal lesion
Empty uterus, adnexal mass, and pelvic free fluid
Intrauterine gestational sac containing a yolk sac
Thick endometrium and no adnexal lesion
subject: ObGy
explanation: In a patient with a positive pregnancy test, an empty uterine cavity together with a separate adnexal mass and free pelvic fluid strongly suggests tubal ectopic pregnancy. A definite extrauterine gestational sac containing a yolk sac or embryo is diagnostic, but the less specific combination in the option still requires clinical correlation. A thick decidualized endometrium can occur with either intrauterine or ectopic pregnancy, and a small central pseudosac can be misleading. Assessment integrates hemodynamic status, pain and bleeding, transvaginal ultrasound, serial quantitative hCG, and repeat imaging when stable. Significant free fluid, shock, or peritoneal signs require urgent surgical evaluation rather than prolonged diagnostic observation.

question: When acute pancreatitis is suspected, which listed serum test is preferred for the biochemical criterion of a value more than three times the upper reference limit?
subject: Medicine
explanation: Serum lipase is the preferred listed biochemical marker for acute pancreatitis because it is generally more pancreas-specific than amylase and remains elevated longer. Diagnosis still requires at least two of three elements: characteristic abdominal pain, lipase or amylase at least three times the upper reference limit, or characteristic imaging. A normal value does not absolutely exclude a late or atypical presentation, and the magnitude above the diagnostic threshold does not reliably grade severity. Bilirubin may suggest biliary obstruction, while creatinine contributes to severity and hydration assessment rather than establishing pancreatitis.

question: Ultrasound shows a solid right intratesticular mass with a reactive hydrocele, and staging demonstrates para-aortic nodes suspicious for metastasis. After serum tumour markers are obtained, what is the appropriate next diagnostic and therapeutic procedure?
subject: Surgery
explanation: The appropriate next step for a suspicious solid intratesticular mass is radical high inguinal orchiectomy after serum AFP, beta-hCG, and LDH are obtained. The inguinal approach permits high cord control and avoids violating scrotal lymphatic drainage. Transscrotal biopsy, scrotal orchiectomy, or simple excision can contaminate a different lymphatic field and compromise staging. Para-aortic nodes are a typical metastatic basin for a right testicular tumour because lymphatic drainage follows the gonadal vessels. A hydrocele can obscure the mass but does not make it benign. Sperm banking and fertility counseling should occur when feasible without unsafe delay. Post-orchiectomy histology, marker kinetics, chest-abdominal-pelvic staging, contralateral testis assessment, and risk factors determine further surveillance or treatment.

question: Why is an inactivated poliovirus-vaccine component incorporated into polio endgame schedules as use of live oral poliovirus vaccine is progressively reduced?
options: It produces stronger intestinal mucosal immunity than oral vaccine
It cannot cause vaccine-associated paralytic poliomyelitis because it contains no live virus
It is given orally and therefore interrupts fecal shedding more effectively
Its attenuated strain has lower neurovirulence than the oral vaccine strain
subject: Medicine
explanation: Inactivated poliovirus vaccine cannot replicate, revert, or cause vaccine-associated paralytic poliomyelitis, which is why an IPV component is important as eradication programmes progressively withdraw live OPV and its rare VAPP and vaccine-derived-virus risks. IPV produces strong systemic antibody and protects against paralysis, while OPV produces stronger intestinal mucosal immunity and is better at limiting fecal transmission. Fractional IPV is one-fifth of the full dose given intradermally; its principal programmatic rationale is dose-sparing during supply constraints. Thus avoidance of live-vaccine paralysis explains the inactivated platform, whereas supply efficiency explains the fractional dose.

question: Which GAG among the following is increased in the mucopolysaccharidosis disease, Natowicz disease?
subject: Medicine
explanation: Natowicz disease is mucopolysaccharidosis type IX, caused by hyaluronidase deficiency and consequent accumulation of hyaluronan, or hyaluronic acid. It is exceptionally rare and has been associated with periarticular soft-tissue masses, joint symptoms, and a relatively mild phenotype compared with many other mucopolysaccharidoses. Hyaluronan is a nonsulfated glycosaminoglycan. Dermatan and heparan sulfate accumulate in disorders such as MPS I and II, predominantly heparan sulfate in MPS III, and keratan sulfate in MPS IV; the enzyme-substrate pairing is what makes hyaluronic acid specific here.

question: Identify the lymphatic structure from the histology section given below.
subject: Pathology
explanation: The image shows thymus, recognized by connective-tissue septa dividing the tissue into lobules, each with a dark lymphocyte-rich cortex and a paler medulla. The medulla contains Hassall corpuscles, concentric whorls of keratinizing thymic epithelial cells, one of which is visible. The thymus supports T-cell maturation, positive selection mainly in the cortex, and negative selection and central tolerance in the medulla. It lacks lymphoid follicles and afferent lymphatics. A lymph node has cortex, paracortex, medullary cords, and sinuses; tonsil has surface epithelium and crypts; spleen has red pulp and white pulp around central arterioles. Age-related fatty involution changes its appearance but does not mean complete functional disappearance.

question: Which listed structure moves inhaled particles proximally from the terminal bronchiolar lumen as part of the mucociliary escalator?
subject: Medicine
explanation: Ciliated epithelial cells extend into the terminal bronchioles and propel surface material proximally toward larger airways, contributing to the mucociliary escalator. Terminal bronchioles contain ciliated cuboidal cells and club cells but normally lack the abundant goblet cells found in larger conducting airways. Type I and type II pneumocytes line alveoli, where deposited particles are handled mainly by alveolar macrophages rather than ciliary transport. Very small respirable particles can therefore evade proximal filtration and reach the acinus, while impaired ciliary function from smoking or genetic disease weakens clearance and promotes infection.
